1	/**
2	 * Plugin system — core types for the Kit/Apparatus model.
3	 *
4	 * Plugins come in two kinds:
5	 * - Kit:       passive package contributing capabilities to consuming apparatuses.
6	 *              No lifecycle, no running state. Read at load time.
7	 * - Apparatus: package contributing persistent running infrastructure.
8	 *              Has a start/stop lifecycle. Receives StartupContext at start.
9	 *
10	 * See: docs/architecture/plugins.md
11	 */
12	
13	// ── Loaded plugin descriptors ──────────────────────────────────────────
14	
15	/** A kit as tracked by the Arbor runtime. */
16	export interface LoadedKit {
17	  readonly packageName: string
18	  readonly id:          string
19	  readonly version:     string
20	  readonly kit:         Kit
21	}
22	
23	/** An apparatus as tracked by the Arbor runtime. */
24	export interface LoadedApparatus {
25	  readonly packageName: string
26	  readonly id:          string
27	  readonly version:     string
28	  readonly apparatus:   Apparatus
29	}
30	
31	/** Union of loaded kit and loaded apparatus. */
32	export type LoadedPlugin = LoadedKit | LoadedApparatus
33	
34	// ── Context types ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
35	
36	/**
37	 * Startup context passed to an apparatus's start(ctx).
38	 *
39	 * Provides lifecycle-event subscription — the only capability that is
40	 * meaningful only during startup. All other guild access (apparatus APIs,
41	 * config, home path, loaded plugins) goes through the `guild()` singleton,
42	 * which is available during start() and in all handlers.
43	 *
44	 * See: docs/architecture/plugins.md
45	 */
46	export interface StartupContext {
47	  /** Subscribe to a guild lifecycle event. Handlers may be async; run sequentially. */
48	  on(event: string, handler: (...args: unknown[]) => void | Promise<void>): void
49	}
50	
51	// ── Kit ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
52	
53	/**
54	 * A kit — passive package contributing capabilities to consuming apparatuses.
55	 * Open record: contribution fields (engines, relays, tools, etc.) are defined
56	 * by the apparatus packages that consume them. `requires` and `recommends` are
57	 * the only framework-level fields.
58	 *
59	 * `requires`: apparatus names whose runtime APIs this kit's contributions depend
60	 *   on at handler invocation time. Hard startup validation failure if a declared
61	 *   apparatus is not installed.
62	 *
63	 * `recommends`: advisory apparatus names — generates startup warnings when
64	 *   expected apparatuses are absent. Not enforced.
65	 */
66	export type Kit = {
67	  requires?:   string[]
68	  recommends?: string[]
69	  [key: string]: unknown
70	}
71	
72	// ── Apparatus ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
73	
74	/**
75	 * An apparatus — package contributing persistent running infrastructure.
76	 * Has a start/stop lifecycle. Receives StartupContext at start.
77	 *
78	 * `requires`: apparatus names that must be started before this apparatus's
79	 *   start() runs. Determines start ordering. Hard startup validation failure
80	 *   if a declared apparatus is not installed.
81	 *
82	 * `recommends`: advisory apparatus names — generates startup warnings when
83	 *   expected apparatuses are absent. Not enforced — the apparatus starts
84	 *   regardless. Use for soft dependencies needed by optional API methods
85	 *   (e.g. The Animator recommends The Loom for summon(), but animate()
86	 *   works without it).
87	 *
88	 * `provides`: the runtime API object this apparatus exposes to other plugins.
89	 *   Retrieved via guild().apparatus<T>(name). Created at manifest-definition time,
90	 *   populated during start.
91	 *
92	 * `supportKit`: kit contributions this apparatus exposes to consuming apparatuses.
93	 *   Treated identically to standalone kit contributions by consumers.
94	 *
95	 * `consumes`: kit contribution field types this apparatus scans for and registers.
96	 *   Enables framework startup warnings when kits contribute types with no consumer.
97	 */
98	export type Apparatus = {
99	  requires?:    string[]
100	  recommends?:  string[]
101	  provides?:    unknown
102	  start:        (ctx: StartupContext) => void | Promise<void>
103	  stop?:        () => void | Promise<void>
104	  supportKit?:  Kit
105	  consumes?:    string[]
106	}
107	
108	// ── Plugin ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
109	
110	/**
111	 * The discriminated union plugin type. A plugin is either a kit or an apparatus.
112	 * The plugin name is always inferred from the npm package name at load time —
113	 * it is never declared in the manifest.
114	 */
115	export type Plugin =
116	  | { kit:       Kit }
117	  | { apparatus: Apparatus }
118	
119	// ── Type guards ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
120	
121	/** Type guard: is this value a kit plugin export? */
122	export function isKit(obj: unknown): obj is { kit: Kit } {
123	  return (
124	    typeof obj === 'object' &&
125	    obj !== null &&
126	    'kit' in obj &&
127	    typeof (obj as { kit: unknown }).kit === 'object' &&
128	    (obj as { kit: unknown }).kit !== null &&
129	    !Array.isArray((obj as { kit: unknown }).kit)
130	  )
131	}
132	
133	/** Type guard: is this value an apparatus plugin export? */
134	export function isApparatus(obj: unknown): obj is { apparatus: Apparatus } {
135	  return (
136	    typeof obj === 'object' &&
137	    obj !== null &&
138	    'apparatus' in obj &&
139	    typeof (obj as { apparatus: unknown }).apparatus === 'object' &&
140	    (obj as { apparatus: unknown }).apparatus !== null &&
141	    typeof (
142	      (obj as { apparatus: Record<string, unknown> }).apparatus.start
143	    ) === 'function'
144	  )
145	}
146	
147	/** Type guard: narrows a LoadedPlugin to LoadedKit. */
148	export function isLoadedKit(p: LoadedPlugin): p is LoadedKit {
149	  return 'kit' in p
150	}
151	
152	/** Type guard: narrows a LoadedPlugin to LoadedApparatus. */
153	export function isLoadedApparatus(p: LoadedPlugin): p is LoadedApparatus {
154	  return 'apparatus' in p
155	}
156

10	
11	## Purpose

13	The Fabricator is the guild's capability catalog. It holds engine design specifications and serves them to the Spider on demand. When the Spider needs to run an engine, it asks the Fabricator for the design by ID — the Fabricator resolves it, the Spider runs it.
14	
15	The Fabricator does **not** execute engines. It does not touch rigs, manage sessions, or interact with the Clerk. It is a pure query service: designs in, designs out.
16	
17	---
18	
19	## Dependencies
20	
21	```
22	requires: []
23	consumes: ['engines']    — scans kit and supportKit contributions for engine designs
24	```
25	
26	---
27	
28	## Engine Design Contract
29	
30	The `@shardworks/fabricator-apparatus` package is the canonical home for the `EngineDesign` interface. Kit authors and apparatus that contribute engines import from this package:
31	
32	```typescript
33	import type { EngineDesign, EngineRunContext, EngineRunResult } from '@shardworks/fabricator-apparatus'
34	```

36	### `EngineDesign`
37	
38	```typescript
39	interface EngineDesign {
40	  /** Unique identifier for this engine design (e.g. 'draft', 'implement', 'review'). */
41	  id: string
42	
43	  /**
44	   * Execute this engine.
45	   *
46	   * Returns 'completed' with yields (synchronous work done inline), or
47	   * 'launched' with a sessionId (async work the Spider polls for).
48	   * The Spider inspects the result shape — no need to declare the kind up front.
49	   *
50	   * @param givens — the engine's declared inputs, assembled by the Spider.
51	   *   A mix of values from the givensSpec (set at rig spawn time, e.g. role,
52	   *   buildCommand, writ) and upstream yields (resolved from completed engines,
53	   *   e.g. draft worktree path). The engine doesn't know or care about
54	   *   provenance — all values arrive the same way.
55	   *
56	   * @param context — minimal execution context. The engine id and an escape hatch
57	   *   (`upstream`) containing all upstream yields keyed by engine id.
58	   *
59	   * Engines pull their own apparatus dependencies via guild().apparatus(...) —
60	   * same pattern as tool handlers.
61	   */
62	  run(givens: Record<string, unknown>, context: EngineRunContext): Promise<EngineRunResult>
63	
64	  /**
65	   * Assemble yields from a completed session.
66	   *
67	   * Called by the Spider's collect step when a quick engine's session
68	   * reaches a terminal state. The engine looks up whatever it needs
69	   * via guild() — same dependency pattern as run().
70	   *
71	   * @param sessionId — the session to collect yields from (primary input).
72	   * @param givens    — same givens that were passed to run().
73	   * @param context   — same execution context that was passed to run().
74	   *
75	   * If not defined, the Spider uses a generic default:
76	   *   { sessionId, sessionStatus, output? }
77	   *
78	   * Only relevant for quick engines (those that return { status: 'launched' }).
79	   * Clockwork engines return yields directly from run().
80	   */
81	  collect?(sessionId: string, givens: Record<string, unknown>, context: EngineRunContext): Promise<unknown>
82	}

# The Fabricator — API Contract

Status: **Draft — MVP**

Package: `@shardworks/fabricator-apparatus` · Plugin id: `fabricator`

> **⚠️ MVP scope.** The first implementation is an engine design registry with kit scanning and a single lookup method. No capability resolution, no need-based queries, no chain composition. The Fabricator earns those features when dynamic rig extension arrives.

---

## Purpose

The Fabricator is the guild's capability catalog. It holds engine design specifications and serves them to the Spider on demand. When the Spider needs to run an engine, it asks the Fabricator for the design by ID — the Fabricator resolves it, the Spider runs it.

The Fabricator does **not** execute engines. It does not touch rigs, manage sessions, or interact with the Clerk. It is a pure query service: designs in, designs out.

---

## Dependencies

```
requires: []
consumes: ['engines']    — scans kit and supportKit contributions for engine designs
```

---

## Engine Design Contract

The `@shardworks/fabricator-apparatus` package is the canonical home for the `EngineDesign` interface. Kit authors and apparatus that contribute engines import from this package:

```typescript
import type { EngineDesign, EngineRunContext, EngineRunResult } from '@shardworks/fabricator-apparatus'
```

### `EngineDesign`

```typescript
interface EngineDesign {
  /** Unique identifier for this engine design (e.g. 'draft', 'implement', 'review'). */
  id: string

  /**
   * Execute this engine.
   *
   * Returns 'completed' with yields (synchronous work done inline), or
   * 'launched' with a sessionId (async work the Spider polls for).
   * The Spider inspects the result shape — no need to declare the kind up front.
   *
   * @param givens — the engine's declared inputs, assembled by the Spider.
   *   A mix of values from the givensSpec (set at rig spawn time, e.g. role,
   *   buildCommand, writ) and upstream yields (resolved from completed engines,
   *   e.g. draft worktree path). The engine doesn't know or care about
   *   provenance — all values arrive the same way.
   *
   * @param context — minimal execution context. The engine id and an escape hatch
   *   (`upstream`) containing all upstream yields keyed by engine id.
   *
   * Engines pull their own apparatus dependencies via guild().apparatus(...) —
   * same pattern as tool handlers.
   */
  run(givens: Record<string, unknown>, context: EngineRunContext): Promise<EngineRunResult>

  /**
   * Assemble yields from a completed session.
   *
   * Called by the Spider's collect step when a quick engine's session
   * reaches a terminal state. The engine looks up whatever it needs
   * via guild() — same dependency pattern as run().
   *
   * @param sessionId — the session to collect yields from (primary input).
   * @param givens    — same givens that were passed to run().
   * @param context   — same execution context that was passed to run().
   *
   * If not defined, the Spider uses a generic default:
   *   { sessionId, sessionStatus, output? }
   *
   * Only relevant for quick engines (those that return { status: 'launched' }).
   * Clockwork engines return yields directly from run().
   */
  collect?(sessionId: string, givens: Record<string, unknown>, context: EngineRunContext): Promise<unknown>
}
```

### `EngineRunContext`

```typescript
interface EngineRunContext {
  /** Simple string identity for this engine instance (e.g. 'draft', 'implement'). */
  engineId: string
  /** All upstream yields, keyed by engine id. Escape hatch for engines that need to inspect the full upstream chain. */
  upstream: Record<string, unknown>
}
```

### `EngineRunResult`

```typescript
type EngineRunResult =
  | { status: 'completed'; yields: unknown }    // clockwork: done, here are the yields

1	# The Clockworks
2	
3	The Clockworks is the guild's nervous system — the event-driven layer that connects things that happen to things that should happen in response. It turns the guild from an imperative system (things happen when someone calls something) into a reactive one (things happen because other things happened).
4	
5	The Clockworks is Pillar 5 of the guild architecture. The first four pillars make the guild *capable*. The Clockworks makes it *alive* — able to act on itself without waiting for the patron to push.
6	
7	---
8	
9	## Core Concepts
10	
11	### Events
12	
13	An event is an immutable fact: *this happened*.
14	
15	```typescript
16	{
17	  name: string;       // e.g. "commission.sealed", "tool.installed"
18	  payload: unknown;   // event-specific data
19	  emitter: string;    // who signaled it: anima name, engine name, or "framework"
20	  firedAt: DateTime;
21	}
22	```
23	
24	Events are persisted to the Clockworks' own event queue immediately when signaled. They do not carry intent — they carry record. An event says "this occurred"; it does not say "therefore do this." That causal link lives in standing orders. The event and dispatch tables are internal Clockworks operational state — not part of the guild's Books (Register, Ledger, Daybook).
25	
26	#### Framework events
27	
28	Signaled automatically by `nexus-core` operations. Always available; no guild configuration needed.
29	
30	| Event | Signaled when |
31	|---|---|
32	| `anima.instantiated` | A new anima is created |
33	| `anima.state.changed` | An anima transitions state (aspirant → active, active → retired) |
34	| `anima.manifested` | An anima is launched for a session |
35	| `anima.session.ended` | A session completes |
36	| `commission.posted` | A new commission is posted by the patron |
37	| `commission.state.changed` | A commission transitions state |
38	| `commission.sealed` | A commission completes successfully |
39	| `commission.failed` | A commission fails |
40	| `{type}.ready` | A writ transitions to `ready` — available for dispatch (e.g. `mandate.ready`, `task.ready`) |
41	| `{type}.completed` | A writ is fulfilled (e.g. `mandate.completed`, `task.completed`) |
42	| `{type}.failed` | A writ fails (e.g. `mandate.failed`, `task.failed`) |
43	| `tool.installed` | A tool (implement, engine, curriculum, or temperament) is installed |
44	| `tool.removed` | A tool is removed |
45	| `migration.applied` | A database migration is applied |
46	| `guild.initialized` | The guild is first initialized |
47	| `standing-order.failed` | A standing order failed during execution (see Error Handling) |
48	
49	Framework events are signaled from authoritative code paths in `nexus-core`. Animas cannot signal them.
50

# The Clockworks

The Clockworks is the guild's nervous system — the event-driven layer that connects things that happen to things that should happen in response. It turns the guild from an imperative system (things happen when someone calls something) into a reactive one (things happen because other things happened).

The Clockworks is Pillar 5 of the guild architecture. The first four pillars make the guild *capable*. The Clockworks makes it *alive* — able to act on itself without waiting for the patron to push.

---

## Core Concepts

### Events

An event is an immutable fact: *this happened*.

```typescript
{
  name: string;       // e.g. "commission.sealed", "tool.installed"
  payload: unknown;   // event-specific data
  emitter: string;    // who signaled it: anima name, engine name, or "framework"
  firedAt: DateTime;
}
```

Events are persisted to the Clockworks' own event queue immediately when signaled. They do not carry intent — they carry record. An event says "this occurred"; it does not say "therefore do this." That causal link lives in standing orders. The event and dispatch tables are internal Clockworks operational state — not part of the guild's Books (Register, Ledger, Daybook).

#### Framework events

Signaled automatically by `nexus-core` operations. Always available; no guild configuration needed.

| Event | Signaled when |
|---|---|
| `anima.instantiated` | A new anima is created |
| `anima.state.changed` | An anima transitions state (aspirant → active, active → retired) |
| `anima.manifested` | An anima is launched for a session |
| `anima.session.ended` | A session completes |
| `commission.posted` | A new commission is posted by the patron |
| `commission.state.changed` | A commission transitions state |
| `commission.sealed` | A commission completes successfully |
| `commission.failed` | A commission fails |
| `{type}.ready` | A writ transitions to `ready` — available for dispatch (e.g. `mandate.ready`, `task.ready`) |
| `{type}.completed` | A writ is fulfilled (e.g. `mandate.completed`, `task.completed`) |
| `{type}.failed` | A writ fails (e.g. `mandate.failed`, `task.failed`) |
| `tool.installed` | A tool (implement, engine, curriculum, or temperament) is installed |
| `tool.removed` | A tool is removed |
| `migration.applied` | A database migration is applied |
| `guild.initialized` | The guild is first initialized |
| `standing-order.failed` | A standing order failed during execution (see Error Handling) |

Framework events are signaled from authoritative code paths in `nexus-core`. Animas cannot signal them.

1	# The Stacks — API Contract
2	
3	Status: **Draft — under review**
4	
5	Package: `@shardworks/stacks` · Plugin id: `stacks`
6	
7	---
8	
9	## Purpose
10	
11	The Stacks is the guild's persistence layer — a JSON document store backed by SQLite, with change data capture (CDC) as its primary integration mechanism. Every piece of guild state that needs to survive process restarts lives here: writs, sessions, anima records, event logs.
12	
13	The Stacks owns the write path exclusively. There is no raw SQL escape hatch, no bypass. This is what makes CDC reliable — if the API is the only write path, the event stream is complete. The Stacks does not know what the documents mean; it stores them, indexes them, watches them, and stays out of the way.
14	
15	---
16	
17	## Dependencies
18	
19	```
20	requires: []
21	consumes: ['books']    — scans kit contributions for book declarations
22	```
23	
24	The Stacks has no apparatus dependencies — it is the foundation layer that everything else builds on.
25	
26	---
27	
28	## Kit Interface
29	
30	When The Stacks is installed, kits gain the ability to declare a `books` field — a record of named book declarations with index schemas. The Stacks reads these at startup and creates or reconciles the backing tables. Schema changes are additive only — new books and new indexes are always safe; nothing is ever dropped automatically.
31	
32	```typescript
33	// Example: a kit declaring two books
34	export default {
35	  kit: {
36	    requires: ['stacks'],
37	    books: {
38	      writs:    { indexes: ['status', 'createdAt', 'parent.id', ['status', 'createdAt']] },
39	      sessions: { indexes: ['writId', 'startedAt', 'animaId'] },
40	    },
41	  },
42	} satisfies Plugin
43	```
44	
45	```typescript
46	interface BookSchema {
47	  /**
48	   * Fields or field tuples to index for efficient querying.
49	   * - A `string` creates a single-field index (e.g. `'status'`)
50	   * - A `string[]` creates a compound index (e.g. `['status', 'createdAt']`)
51	   *
52	   * Dot-notation for nested fields ('parent.id') is supported.
53	   */
54	  indexes?: (string | string[])[]
55	}
56	```
57	
58	> **Index policy:** Only declared indexes are guaranteed to be efficient. Querying on a non-indexed field works but may scan the full table.
59	
60	---
61	
62	## `StacksApi` Interface (`provides`)
63	
64	```typescript
65	interface StacksApi {
66	  /**
67	   * Get a writable Book handle for the given owner and book name.
68	   *
69	   * `ownerId` is the plugin id of the declaring kit — this is the write
70	   * boundary. Trust-based: not validated at runtime against the caller's
71	   * identity. `readBook()` enforces the boundary at the type level.
72	   */
73	  book<T extends BookEntry>(ownerId: string, name: string): Book<T>
74	
75	  /**
76	   * Get a read-only Book handle scoped to another plugin's book.
77	   * Exposes `get`, `find`, `list`, and `count` only.
78	   */
79	  readBook<T extends BookEntry>(ownerId: string, name: string): ReadOnlyBook<T>
80

# The Stacks — API Contract

Status: **Draft — under review**

Package: `@shardworks/stacks` · Plugin id: `stacks`

---

## Purpose

The Stacks is the guild's persistence layer — a JSON document store backed by SQLite, with change data capture (CDC) as its primary integration mechanism. Every piece of guild state that needs to survive process restarts lives here: writs, sessions, anima records, event logs.

The Stacks owns the write path exclusively. There is no raw SQL escape hatch, no bypass. This is what makes CDC reliable — if the API is the only write path, the event stream is complete. The Stacks does not know what the documents mean; it stores them, indexes them, watches them, and stays out of the way.

---

## Dependencies

```
requires: []
consumes: ['books']    — scans kit contributions for book declarations
```

The Stacks has no apparatus dependencies — it is the foundation layer that everything else builds on.

---

## Kit Interface

When The Stacks is installed, kits gain the ability to declare a `books` field — a record of named book declarations with index schemas. The Stacks reads these at startup and creates or reconciles the backing tables. Schema changes are additive only — new books and new indexes are always safe; nothing is ever dropped automatically.

```typescript
// Example: a kit declaring two books
export default {
  kit: {
    requires: ['stacks'],
    books: {
      writs:    { indexes: ['status', 'createdAt', 'parent.id', ['status', 'createdAt']] },
      sessions: { indexes: ['writId', 'startedAt', 'animaId'] },
    },
  },
} satisfies Plugin
```

```typescript
interface BookSchema {
  /**
   * Fields or field tuples to index for efficient querying.
   * - A `string` creates a single-field index (e.g. `'status'`)
   * - A `string[]` creates a compound index (e.g. `['status', 'createdAt']`)
   *
   * Dot-notation for nested fields ('parent.id') is supported.
   */
  indexes?: (string | string[])[]
}
```

> **Index policy:** Only declared indexes are guaranteed to be efficient. Querying on a non-indexed field works but may scan the full table.

---

## `StacksApi` Interface (`provides`)

```typescript
interface StacksApi {
  /**
   * Get a writable Book handle for the given owner and book name.
   *
   * `ownerId` is the plugin id of the declaring kit — this is the write
   * boundary. Trust-based: not validated at runtime against the caller's
   * identity. `readBook()` enforces the boundary at the type level.
   */
  book<T extends BookEntry>(ownerId: string, name: string): Book<T>

  /**
   * Get a read-only Book handle scoped to another plugin's book.
   * Exposes `get`, `find`, `list`, and `count` only.
   */
  readBook<T extends BookEntry>(ownerId: string, name: string): ReadOnlyBook<T>

81	  /**
82	   * Register a CDC handler for a book.
83	   *
84	   * Must be called during startup before any writes occur.
85	   * The `failOnError` option controls execution phase — see
86	   * "Change Data Capture" below.
87	   */
88	  watch<T extends BookEntry>(
89	    ownerId: string,
90	    bookName: string,
91	    handler: ChangeHandler<T>,
92	    options?: WatchOptions,
93	  ): void
94	
95	  /**
96	   * Execute a function within an atomic transaction.
97	   *
98	   * All writes inside `fn` commit or roll back together. Reads see
99	   * uncommitted writes (read-your-writes). CDC events are buffered
100	   * and fired (coalesced per-document) after commit.
101	   */
102	  transaction<R>(fn: (tx: TransactionContext) => Promise<R>): Promise<R>
103	}
104	
105	interface TransactionContext {
106	  book<T extends BookEntry>(ownerId: string, name: string): Book<T>
107	  readBook<T extends BookEntry>(ownerId: string, name: string): ReadOnlyBook<T>
108	}
109	```
110	
111	---
112	
113	## Configuration
114	
115	```json
116	{
117	  "stacks": {
118	    "autoMigrate": true,
119	    "maxCascadeDepth": 16
120	  }
121	}
122	```
123	
124	- **`autoMigrate`** — whether to apply database migrations automatically on startup.
125	- **`maxCascadeDepth`** — maximum CDC cascade depth before the transaction is aborted (default: 16).
126	
127	---
128	
129	## Document Model
130	
131	A **book** is a named collection of documents. Every document must include an `id: string` field. The framework puts nothing else in the envelope — no `_rev`, no `_createdAt`, no `_type`. Domain types own their own fields.
132	
133	```typescript
134	type BookEntry = { id: string } & Record<string, unknown>
135	```
136	
137	IDs are author-generated. Plugins own ID generation (ULIDs recommended). The Stacks has no opinion on format beyond requiring a non-empty string. Documents are stored as plain JSON objects; nested objects are fully supported. Field names in query predicates use dot-notation for nested access (`'parent.id'`).
138	
139	---
140	
141	## Read and Write API
142	
143	```typescript
144	interface Book<T extends BookEntry> extends ReadOnlyBook<T> {
145	  /** Upsert a document. Fires a `create` or `update` CDC event. */
146	  put(entry: T): Promise<void>
147	
148	  /**
149	   * Partially update a document (top-level field merge).
150	   * Throws if the document does not exist. Returns the full document after merge.
151	   * Fires an `update` CDC event with the pre-patch document as `prev`.
152	   */
153	  patch(id: string, fields: Partial<Omit<T, 'id'>>): Promise<T>
154	
155	  /** Delete by id. Silent no-op if the document does not exist. */
156	  delete(id: string): Promise<void>
157	}
158	
159	interface ReadOnlyBook<T extends BookEntry> {
160	  get(id: string): Promise<T | null>
161	  find(query: BookQuery<T>): Promise<T[]>
162	  list(options?: ListOptions): Promise<T[]>
163	  count(where?: WhereClause<T> | { or: WhereClause<T>[] }): Promise<number>
164	}
165	```
166	
167	---
168	
169	## Query Language
170	
171	Where conditions are expressed as tuples — `[field, operator, value?]`. All conditions within a single `WhereClause` are AND-ed.
172	
173	```typescript
174	type WhereCondition<T> =
175	  | [string, '=' | '!=', Scalar]
176	  | [string, '>' | '>=' | '<' | '<=', number | string]
177	  | [string, 'LIKE', string]       // % and _ wildcards
178	  | [string, 'IN', Scalar[]]
179	  | [string, 'IS NULL' | 'IS NOT NULL']
180	
181	type Scalar = string | number | boolean | null
182	type WhereClause<T> = WhereCondition<T>[]
183	```
184	
185	**OR support:** The `where` field accepts `{ or: WhereClause<T>[] }` — each element is an AND-clause; results are unioned and deduplicated by `id`.
186	
187	**Sorting:** Multi-field ordering via `orderBy: [field, 'asc' | 'desc']` or an array of such tuples.
188	
189	**Pagination:** `{ limit: number; offset?: number }`. Offset requires limit.
190	
191	```typescript
192	type BookQuery<T extends BookEntry> = {
193	  where?:   WhereClause<T> | { or: WhereClause<T>[] }
194	  orderBy?: OrderBy
195	} & Pagination
196	```
197	
198	---
199

/**
   * Register a CDC handler for a book.
   *
   * Must be called during startup before any writes occur.
   * The `failOnError` option controls execution phase — see
   * "Change Data Capture" below.
   */
  watch<T extends BookEntry>(
    ownerId: string,
    bookName: string,
    handler: ChangeHandler<T>,
    options?: WatchOptions,
  ): void

  /**
   * Execute a function within an atomic transaction.
   *
   * All writes inside `fn` commit or roll back together. Reads see
   * uncommitted writes (read-your-writes). CDC events are buffered
   * and fired (coalesced per-document) after commit.
   */
  transaction<R>(fn: (tx: TransactionContext) => Promise<R>): Promise<R>
}

interface TransactionContext {
  book<T extends BookEntry>(ownerId: string, name: string): Book<T>
  readBook<T extends BookEntry>(ownerId: string, name: string): ReadOnlyBook<T>
}
```

---

## Configuration

```json
{
  "stacks": {
    "autoMigrate": true,
    "maxCascadeDepth": 16
  }
}
```

- **`autoMigrate`** — whether to apply database migrations automatically on startup.
- **`maxCascadeDepth`** — maximum CDC cascade depth before the transaction is aborted (default: 16).

---

## Document Model

A **book** is a named collection of documents. Every document must include an `id: string` field. The framework puts nothing else in the envelope — no `_rev`, no `_createdAt`, no `_type`. Domain types own their own fields.

```typescript
type BookEntry = { id: string } & Record<string, unknown>
```

IDs are author-generated. Plugins own ID generation (ULIDs recommended). The Stacks has no opinion on format beyond requiring a non-empty string. Documents are stored as plain JSON objects; nested objects are fully supported. Field names in query predicates use dot-notation for nested access (`'parent.id'`).

---

## Read and Write API

```typescript
interface Book<T extends BookEntry> extends ReadOnlyBook<T> {
  /** Upsert a document. Fires a `create` or `update` CDC event. */
  put(entry: T): Promise<void>

  /**
   * Partially update a document (top-level field merge).
   * Throws if the document does not exist. Returns the full document after merge.
   * Fires an `update` CDC event with the pre-patch document as `prev`.
   */
  patch(id: string, fields: Partial<Omit<T, 'id'>>): Promise<T>

  /** Delete by id. Silent no-op if the document does not exist. */
  delete(id: string): Promise<void>
}

interface ReadOnlyBook<T extends BookEntry> {
  get(id: string): Promise<T | null>
  find(query: BookQuery<T>): Promise<T[]>
  list(options?: ListOptions): Promise<T[]>
  count(where?: WhereClause<T> | { or: WhereClause<T>[] }): Promise<number>
}
```

---

## Query Language

Where conditions are expressed as tuples — `[field, operator, value?]`. All conditions within a single `WhereClause` are AND-ed.

```typescript
type WhereCondition<T> =
  | [string, '=' | '!=', Scalar]
  | [string, '>' | '>=' | '<' | '<=', number | string]
  | [string, 'LIKE', string]       // % and _ wildcards
  | [string, 'IN', Scalar[]]
  | [string, 'IS NULL' | 'IS NOT NULL']

type Scalar = string | number | boolean | null
type WhereClause<T> = WhereCondition<T>[]
```

**OR support:** The `where` field accepts `{ or: WhereClause<T>[] }` — each element is an AND-clause; results are unioned and deduplicated by `id`.

**Sorting:** Multi-field ordering via `orderBy: [field, 'asc' | 'desc']` or an array of such tuples.

**Pagination:** `{ limit: number; offset?: number }`. Offset requires limit.

```typescript
type BookQuery<T extends BookEntry> = {
  where?:   WhereClause<T> | { or: WhereClause<T>[] }
  orderBy?: OrderBy
} & Pagination
```

---

200	## Change Data Capture
201	
202	All writes go through the Stacks API — this is the guarantee that makes CDC complete. CDC handlers fire on every write to a watched book.
203	
204	### Event shapes
205	
206	```typescript
207	type ChangeEvent<T extends BookEntry> =
208	  | CreateEvent<T>
209	  | UpdateEvent<T>
210	  | DeleteEvent<T>
211	
212	interface CreateEvent<T> {
213	  type: 'create'; ownerId: string; book: string; entry: T
214	}
215	interface UpdateEvent<T> {
216	  type: 'update'; ownerId: string; book: string; entry: T; prev: T
217	}
218	interface DeleteEvent<T> {
219	  type: 'delete'; ownerId: string; book: string; id: string; prev: T
220	}
221	```
222	
223	`prev` is always populated for `update` and `delete` events. The pre-read cost is only paid when handlers are registered for the book.
224	
225	### Two-phase execution
226	
227	```typescript
228	type ChangeHandler<T extends BookEntry> = (event: ChangeEvent<T>) => Promise<void> | void
229	
230	interface WatchOptions {
231	  /**
232	   * true  (default) — Phase 1: runs INSIDE the transaction. Handler writes
233	   *   join the same atomic unit. If the handler throws, everything rolls back.
234	   * false — Phase 2: runs AFTER commit. Data is persisted. Handler failures
235	   *   are logged as warnings.
236	   */
237	  failOnError?: boolean
238	}
239	```
240	
241	**Phase 1 — Cascade** (`failOnError: true`, the default). Runs inside the transaction, before commit. The handler's writes join the same atomic unit. If the handler throws, everything rolls back — the triggering write, the handler's writes, and all nested cascades. This is the correct phase for referential integrity (e.g. cancelling child writs when a parent is cancelled).
242	
243	**Phase 2 — Notification** (`failOnError: false`). Runs after the transaction commits. Data is already persisted. This is the correct phase for Clockworks event emission, telemetry, and audit logging. If your Phase 1 handler produces effects outside the Stacks, it probably belongs in Phase 2 — transaction rollback cannot undo non-database side effects.
244	
245	### Transaction binding
246	
247	Handlers access the Stacks through the normal `guild().apparatus<StacksApi>('stacks')` path. Transaction binding is transparent via `AsyncLocalStorage` — Phase 1 handlers automatically route their book operations through the active transaction. No special API, no transaction-aware handles. The transaction context is ambient.
248	
249	**All book operations inside a Phase 1 handler must be `await`-ed.** A non-awaited write inherits the transaction context but may execute after commit or rollback, producing undefined behavior.
250	
251	### Cascade depth limiting
252	
253	A depth counter prevents infinite recursion from accidental handler cycles. Default limit is 16, configurable via `"stacks": { "maxCascadeDepth": 32 }` in `guild.json`. Exceeding the limit throws and rolls back the entire transaction.
254	
255	### CDC event coalescing
256	
257	Within a transaction, multiple writes to the same document produce a single CDC event reflecting the net change:
258	
259	| Mutations | Coalesced event |
260	|---|---|
261	| create | `create` (final state) |
262	| create → update(s) | `create` (final state) |
263	| create → delete | *(no event)* |
264	| update(s) | `update` (pre-transaction → final) |
265	| update → delete | `delete` (pre-transaction state) |
266	| delete | `delete` (pre-transaction state) |
267	
268	Phase 2 handlers see exactly one event per document. They never see intermediate states.
269	
270	---
271	
272	## Transaction Model
273	
274	Every write participates in a transaction. There are two ways they're created:
275	
276	**Implicit.** Every `put()`, `patch()`, or `delete()` outside a transaction opens one implicitly. It spans the write plus all Phase 1 handlers (and their cascades). Commits after all Phase 1 handlers succeed; rolls back if any throw.
277	
278	**Explicit.** `stacks.transaction()` groups multiple writes into a single atomic unit. Phase 1 handlers within an explicit transaction join the same transaction. Commit is deferred until the callback completes.
279	
280	Reads within a transaction see uncommitted writes from the same transaction (read-your-writes).
281	
282	---
283	
284	## Backend Interface
285	
286	The Stacks depends on a `StacksBackend` interface, not SQLite directly. The default implementation uses SQLite via `better-sqlite3`; alternative backends implement the same interface. No SQLite types leak into the public API.
287	
288	```typescript
289	interface StacksBackend {
290	  open(options: BackendOptions): Promise<void>
291	  close(): Promise<void>
292	  ensureBook(ref: BookRef, schema: BookSchema): Promise<void>
293	  beginTransaction(): Promise<BackendTransaction>
294	}
295	
296	interface BackendTransaction {
297	  put(ref: BookRef, entry: BookEntry, opts?: { withPrev: boolean }): Promise<PutResult>
298	  patch(ref: BookRef, id: string, fields: Record<string, unknown>): Promise<PatchResult>
299	  delete(ref: BookRef, id: string, opts?: { withPrev: boolean }): Promise<DeleteResult>

file.content:
## Change Data Capture

All writes go through the Stacks API — this is the guarantee that makes CDC complete. CDC handlers fire on every write to a watched book.

### Event shapes

```typescript
type ChangeEvent<T extends BookEntry> =
  | CreateEvent<T>
  | UpdateEvent<T>
  | DeleteEvent<T>

interface CreateEvent<T> {
  type: 'create'; ownerId: string; book: string; entry: T
}
interface UpdateEvent<T> {
  type: 'update'; ownerId: string; book: string; entry: T; prev: T
}
interface DeleteEvent<T> {
  type: 'delete'; ownerId: string; book: string; id: string; prev: T
}
```

`prev` is always populated for `update` and `delete` events. The pre-read cost is only paid when handlers are registered for the book.

### Two-phase execution

```typescript
type ChangeHandler<T extends BookEntry> = (event: ChangeEvent<T>) => Promise<void> | void

interface WatchOptions {
  /**
   * true  (default) — Phase 1: runs INSIDE the transaction. Handler writes
   *   join the same atomic unit. If the handler throws, everything rolls back.
   * false — Phase 2: runs AFTER commit. Data is persisted. Handler failures
   *   are logged as warnings.
   */
  failOnError?: boolean
}
```

**Phase 1 — Cascade** (`failOnError: true`, the default). Runs inside the transaction, before commit. The handler's writes join the same atomic unit. If the handler throws, everything rolls back — the triggering write, the handler's writes, and all nested cascades. This is the correct phase for referential integrity (e.g. cancelling child writs when a parent is cancelled).

**Phase 2 — Notification** (`failOnError: false`). Runs after the transaction commits. Data is already persisted. This is the correct phase for Clockworks event emission, telemetry, and audit logging. If your Phase 1 handler produces effects outside the Stacks, it probably belongs in Phase 2 — transaction rollback cannot undo non-database side effects.

### Transaction binding

Handlers access the Stacks through the normal `guild().apparatus<StacksApi>('stacks')` path. Transaction binding is transparent via `AsyncLocalStorage` — Phase 1 handlers automatically route their book operations through the active transaction. No special API, no transaction-aware handles. The transaction context is ambient.

**All book operations inside a Phase 1 handler must be `await`-ed.** A non-awaited write inherits the transaction context but may execute after commit or rollback, producing undefined behavior.

### Cascade depth limiting

A depth counter prevents infinite recursion from accidental handler cycles. Default limit is 16, configurable via `"stacks": { "maxCascadeDepth": 32 }` in `guild.json`. Exceeding the limit throws and rolls back the entire transaction.

### CDC event coalescing

Within a transaction, multiple writes to the same document produce a single CDC event reflecting the net change:

| Mutations | Coalesced event |
|---|---|
| create | `create` (final state) |
| create → update(s) | `create` (final state) |
| create → delete | *(no event)* |
| update(s) | `update` (pre-transaction → final) |
| update → delete | `delete` (pre-transaction state) |
| delete | `delete` (pre-transaction state) |

Phase 2 handlers see exactly one event per document. They never see intermediate states.

---

## Transaction Model

Every write participates in a transaction. There are two ways they're created:

**Implicit.** Every `put()`, `patch()`, or `delete()` outside a transaction opens one implicitly. It spans the write plus all Phase 1 handlers (and their cascades). Commits after all Phase 1 handlers succeed; rolls back if any throw.

**Explicit.** `stacks.transaction()` groups multiple writes into a single atomic unit. Phase 1 handlers within an explicit transaction join the same transaction. Commit is deferred until the callback completes.

Reads within a transaction see uncommitted writes from the same transaction (read-your-writes).

---

## Backend Interface

The Stacks depends on a `StacksBackend` interface, not SQLite directly. The default implementation uses SQLite via `better-sqlite3`; alternative backends implement the same interface. No SQLite types leak into the public API.

```typescript
interface StacksBackend {
  open(options: BackendOptions): Promise<void>
  close(): Promise<void>
  ensureBook(ref: BookRef, schema: BookSchema): Promise<void>
  beginTransaction(): Promise<BackendTransaction>
}

interface BackendTransaction {
  put(ref: BookRef, entry: BookEntry, opts?: { withPrev: boolean }): Promise<PutResult>
  patch(ref: BookRef, id: string, fields: Record<string, unknown>): Promise<PatchResult>
  delete(ref: BookRef, id: string, opts?: { withPrev: boolean }): Promise<DeleteResult>

file.content:
# Plugin Architecture

This document describes the plugin system — how the guild's capabilities are packaged, installed, and composed. For the broader system context, see [overview.md](overview.md).

---

## Overview

The guild framework ships with no running infrastructure of its own. The Clockworks, the Spider, the Surveyor — everything that makes a guild operational is contributed by plugins. `nsg init` installs a default plugin set; a guild's installed plugins determine what it can do.

This is a deliberate design choice. Keeping the framework core to a plugin loader and a set of type contracts means each piece of infrastructure is independently testable, replaceable, and comprehensible. There is no privileged built-in layer; a core apparatus and a community kit are the same kind of thing.

Plugins come in two kinds:

- **Kits** — passive packages contributing capabilities to consuming apparatuses. No lifecycle, no running state. Read at load time and forwarded to consuming apparatuses.
- **Apparatuses** — packages contributing persistent running infrastructure. Have a `start`/`stop` lifecycle. May include a `supportKit` that exposes their capabilities to the rest of the guild.

**Plugin** is retained as a framework-internal and technical term for "either of the above." It appears in error messages, internal types, and npm package conventions, but is not the primary vocabulary users encounter. The guild vocabulary is Kit and Apparatus.

---

## Kit

A kit is a passive package contributing capabilities to the guild. Kits have no lifecycle — they are read at load time and their contributions are forwarded to consuming apparatuses. Nothing about a kit participates in `start`/`stop` or requires a running system.

```typescript
type Kit = {
  requires?:   string[]
  recommends?: string[]
  [key: string]:  unknown
}
```

A kit is an open record. The contribution fields (`relays`, `engines`, `tools`, or anything else) are defined by the apparatus packages that consume them, not by the framework. `requires` and `recommends` are the only framework-level fields.

**`requires`** is an array of apparatus names whose runtime APIs this kit's contributions depend on at handler invocation time. If a tool contributed by this kit calls `guild().apparatus("books")`, the kit must declare `requires: ["books"]`. Validated at startup — if a declared apparatus is not installed, the guild refuses to start with a specific error. Hard failure, not advisory.

**`recommends`** is an advisory list of apparatus names the kit's contributions are most useful with, used to generate startup warnings when expected apparatuses are absent. Not enforced.

A kit package exports its manifest as the default export:

```typescript
import type { ClockworksKit } from "nexus-clockworks"
import type { SpiderKit }     from "nexus-spider"
import type { AnimaKit }      from "nexus-sessions"

export default {
  kit: {
    requires:   ["nexus-books"],
    recommends: ["nexus-clockworks", "nexus-spider"],

1	# Building Engines
2	
3	This guide explains how to build clockwork engines — event-driven handlers that respond to guild events via standing orders. For building interactive tools that animas wield, see [Building Tools](building-tools.md).
4	
5	## What engines are
6	
7	Engines are automated processes with no AI involvement. They run deterministic logic in response to events: rolling up completion status, dispatching work, transforming data, enforcing policies. They don't have instruction documents because no anima wields them — they're guild infrastructure.
8	
9	Engines are wired to events through **standing orders** in `guild.json`. When an event fires, the Clockworks runner finds matching standing orders and calls the engine's handler with the event.
10	
11	## Quick start
12	
13	An engine is a package with these files:
14	
15	```
16	my-engine/
17	  package.json              ← npm package metadata
18	  nexus-engine.json         ← Nexus descriptor
19	  src/
20	    handler.ts              ← the engine handler (default export)
21	```
22	
23	### The handler
24	
25	Use the `engine()` factory from `@shardworks/nexus-core`:
26	
27	```typescript
28	import { engine } from '@shardworks/nexus-core';
29	
30	export default engine({
31	  name: 'my-engine',
32	  handler: async (event, { home, params }) => {
33	    // event  — the GuildEvent that triggered this engine (or null for direct invocation)
34	    // home   — absolute path to the guild root
35	    // params — extra keys from the standing order that invoked this engine
36	
37	    if (!event) return; // nothing to do without an event
38	
39	    console.log(`Handling ${event.name}`, event.payload);
40	
41	    // Do your work here...
42	  }
43	});
44	```
45	
46	### Key rules
47	
48	1. **Default export.** The engine must be the default export. The Clockworks runner does `import(modulePath)` and reads `.default`.
49	2. **Async handler.** Engine handlers must return `Promise<void>`. The runner `await`s the handler.
50	3. **Event may be null.** When invoked directly (not via a standing order), `event` is `null`. Guard accordingly.
51	4. **Throw for errors.** If the handler throws, the Clockworks runner catches the error, records a failed dispatch, and signals `standing-order.failed`.
52	5. **Use `home` for everything.** The guild root is your entry point to all guild state — database, config, file system.
53	6. **Params are untyped.** `params` is `Record<string, unknown>` — cast to expected types in your handler. Provide sensible defaults.
54	
55	### `nexus-engine.json`
56	
57	```json
58	{
59	  "entry": "src/handler.ts",
60	  "version": "0.1.0",
61	  "description": "What this engine does"
62	}
63	```
64	
65	Fields:
66	- `entry` — (required) path to the handler module
67	- `version` — informational, recorded in guild.json `upstream`
68	- `description` — human-readable
69	
70	### `package.json`
71	
72	```json
73	{
74	  "name": "@shardworks/engine-my-engine",
75	  "version": "0.1.0",
76	  "type": "module",
77	  "exports": {
78	    ".": "./src/handler.ts"
79	  },
80	  "dependencies": {
81	    "@shardworks/nexus-core": "workspace:*"
82	  }
83	}
84	```
85	
86	Engines don't need `zod` (no parameter validation) or instruction files (no anima interaction).
87	
88	## Standing order wiring
89	
90	Engines are connected to events through standing orders in `guild.json`:
91	
92	```json
93	{
94	  "clockworks": {
95	    "standingOrders": [
96	      { "on": "session.ended", "run": "my-engine" },
97	      { "on": "task.completed", "run": "my-engine" }
98	    ]
99	  }
100	}
101	```
102	
103	An engine can respond to multiple events. Multiple engines can respond to the same event — they execute in declaration order.
104	
105	### Passing params to engines
106	
107	Any key on a standing order that isn't `on` or `run` is passed to the engine as a param:
108	
109	```json
110	{ "on": "deploy.requested", "run": "deploy", "environment": "staging", "dryRun": true }
111	```
112	
113	The engine receives these in `params`:
114	
115	```typescript
116	handler: async (event, { home, params }) => {
117	  const environment = (params.environment as string) ?? 'production';
118	  const dryRun = (params.dryRun as boolean) ?? false;
119	}
120	```
121	
122	This lets the same engine serve multiple standing orders with different configurations. Params default to `{}` when no extra keys are present.
123	
124	The engine must also be registered in guild.json's `engines` registry:
125	
126	```json
127	{
128	  "engines": {
129	    "my-engine": {
130	      "upstream": "@shardworks/engine-my-engine@0.1.0",
131	      "installedAt": "2026-03-25T00:00:00.000Z",
132	      "package": "@shardworks/engine-my-engine"
133	    }
134	  }
135	}
136	```
137	
138	This happens automatically when installed via `nsg tool install`.
139	
140	## Reading guild state from an engine
141	
142	Engines have full access to `@shardworks/nexus-core`. Common patterns:
143	
144	### Reading event payloads
145	
146	```typescript
147	handler: async (event, { home }) => {
148	  if (!event) return;
149	
150	  // Event payloads are typed as `unknown` — cast based on the event name
151	  const payload = event.payload as { writId: string } | null;
152	  if (!payload?.writId) return;
153	
154	  // Now use the writId...
155	}
156	```
157	
158	### Querying the database
159	
160	```typescript
161	import { showWrit, listWrits, readGuildConfig } from '@shardworks/nexus-core';
162	
163	handler: async (event, { home }) => {
164	  const writ = showWrit(home, writId);
165	  if (!writ) return;
166	
167	  const children = listWrits(home, { parentId: writ.id });
168	  const config = readGuildConfig(home);
169	  // ...
170	}
171	```
172	
173	### Writing to the database
174	
175	```typescript
176	import { completeWrit, signalEvent } from '@shardworks/nexus-core';
177	
178	handler: async (event, { home }) => {
179	  // Complete a writ — this automatically signals {type}.completed
180	  completeWrit(home, writId);
181	}
182	```
183	
184	## Signaling follow-on events
185	
186	Engines can signal events to trigger further automation (event chaining):
187	
188	```typescript
189	import { signalEvent } from '@shardworks/nexus-core';
190	
191	handler: async (event, { home }) => {
192	  // Do some work...
193	
194	  // Signal a custom event for downstream processing
195	  signalEvent(home, 'deploy.ready', { version: '1.2.3' }, 'my-engine');
196	}
197	```
198	
199	**Important:** Writ lifecycle events (like `task.completed`) are signaled automatically by `completeWrit()` and `failWrit()`. Don't double-signal — just call the appropriate function and the event fires.
200	
201	For custom events, you must declare them in `guild.json` first if animas need to signal them. Engines can signal framework events directly (they call `signalEvent()`, which doesn't go through `validateCustomEvent()`).
202	
203	## Error handling
204	
205	### The `standing-order.failed` safety net
206	
207	When an engine handler throws, the Clockworks runner:
208	
209	1. Catches the error
210	2. Records a failed dispatch in `event_dispatches` (with the error message)
211	3. Signals `standing-order.failed` with the original event, the standing order, and the error
212	
213	You can wire a standing order to `standing-order.failed` for alerting:
214	
215	```json
216	{ "on": "standing-order.failed", "brief": "steward" }
217	```
218	
219	**Loop guard:** If processing a `standing-order.failed` event itself fails, the runner stops — it won't cascade infinitely.
220	
221	### Best practices
222	
223	- **Fail fast.** Throw with a clear error message. Don't swallow errors silently.
224	- **Idempotency.** Design handlers to be safe to retry. The same event might be processed again if the runner is restarted.
225	- **Guard against missing data.** Event payloads may be incomplete. Check for null/undefined before accessing fields.
226	
227	## Engine collections
228	
229	A single package can export multiple engines:
230	
231	```typescript
232	import { engine } from '@shardworks/nexus-core';
233	
234	export default [
235	  engine({
236	    name: 'writ-notify',
237	    handler: async (event, { home }) => { /* ... */ }
238	  }),
239	  engine({
240	    name: 'writ-audit',
241	    handler: async (event, { home }) => { /* ... */ }
242	  }),
243	];
244	```
245	
246	Each engine in the array is resolved by name. Register each one separately in guild.json with the same `package` but different names.
247	
248	## Installing engines
249	
250	Same as tools — use `nsg tool install`:
251	
252	```bash
253	nsg tool install @shardworks/engine-my-engine
254	```
255	
256	The installer detects `nexus-engine.json` and registers in the `engines` section of guild.json (not `tools`). All five install types work: registry, git-url, workshop, tarball, link.
257	
258	## Testing engines
259	
260	Engine handlers can be called directly in tests:
261	
262	```typescript
263	import { describe, it, beforeEach } from 'node:test';
264	import assert from 'node:assert/strict';
265	import { initGuild, createWrit, completeWrit, showWrit } from '@shardworks/nexus-core';
266	import myEngine from './handler.ts';
267	
268	describe('my-engine', () => {
269	  let home: string;
270	
271	  beforeEach(() => {
272	    home = '/tmp/test-guild-' + Date.now();
273	    initGuild(home, 'test-guild', 'test-model');
274	  });
275	
276	  it('processes a completed writ event', async () => {
277	    // Set up test data
278	    const writ = createWrit(home, { type: 'task', title: 'Test task' });
279	    completeWrit(home, writ.id);
280	
281	    // Simulate the event
282	    await myEngine.handler(
283	      {
284	        id: 'evt-test',
285	        name: 'task.completed',
286	        payload: { writId: writ.id },
287	        emitter: 'framework',
288	        firedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
289	      },
290	      { home, params: {} }
291	    );
292	
293	    // Verify the result
294	    const updated = showWrit(home, writ.id);
295	    assert.equal(updated?.status, 'completed');
296	  });
297	});
298	```
299	
300	For integration testing with the full Clockworks, use `clockTick()`:
301	
302	```typescript
303	import { clockTick, signalEvent } from '@shardworks/nexus-core';
304	
305	// Signal an event
306	signalEvent(home, 'task.completed', { writId: 'wrt-test' }, 'test');
307	
308	// Process it through the Clockworks (requires standing orders in guild.json)
309	const result = await clockTick(home);
310	assert.equal(result?.eventName, 'task.completed');
311	```
312	
313	## Reference implementation: notification engine
314	
315	A concrete engine that sends a notification when a mandate completes — a simple pattern for post-completion automation.
316	
317	```typescript
318	import { engine, showWrit, readCommission } from '@shardworks/nexus-core';
319	
320	export default engine({
321	  name: 'mandate-notify',
322	  handler: async (event, { home }) => {
323	    if (!event) return;
324	
325	    // This engine responds to mandate.completed events
326	    const payload = event.payload as { writId?: string; commissionId?: string } | null;
327	    if (!payload?.writId) return;
328	
329	    const writ = showWrit(home, payload.writId);
330	    if (!writ) return;
331	
332	    // Look up the commission for context
333	    const commission = payload.commissionId
334	      ? readCommission(home, payload.commissionId)
335	      : null;
336	
337	    console.log(`Mandate "${writ.title}" completed for commission ${commission?.id ?? 'unknown'}`);
338	    // In a real engine: send a Slack message, write a summary, etc.
339	  }
340	});
341	```
342	
343	Wire it in guild.json:
344	
345	```json
346	{
347	  "clockworks": {
348	    "standingOrders": [
349	      { "on": "mandate.completed", "run": "mandate-notify" }
350	    ]
351	  }
352	}
353	```
354	
355	**Note:** Completion rollup for writs is handled automatically by the framework. When all children of a writ complete, the parent transitions from `pending` → `ready` (or auto-completes). You don't need a custom engine for rollup — the framework does it internally when `completeWrit()` or `failWrit()` is called.
356	
357	## Further reading
358	
359	- [Core API Reference](../reference/core-api.md) — full function signatures for all imports
360	- [Event Catalog](../reference/event-catalog.md) — every framework event, payload shapes, standing order types
361	- [Schema Reference](../reference/schema.md) — database tables, status lifecycles, entity relationships
362	- [The Clockworks](../architecture/clockworks.md) — architectural overview of the event processing system
363

| { status: 'completed'; yields: unknown }    // clockwork: done, here are the yields
  | { status: 'launched'; sessionId: string }    // quick: session launched, Spider will poll
```

---

## Kit Contribution

Kits contribute engine designs via an `engines` field:

```typescript
export default {
  kit: {
    requires: ['fabricator'],
    engines: {
      draft:     draftEngine,
      implement: implementEngine,
      review:    reviewEngine,
    },
  },
} satisfies Plugin
```

Each value is an `EngineDesign`. The Fabricator scans these contributions reactively via `plugin:initialized` at startup — the same pattern the Instrumentarium uses for tools. See the [Instrumentarium spec](instrumentarium.md) for the reference implementation of kit-contribution scanning.

---

## Support Kit

None. No books, no tools. The Fabricator is a pure in-memory registry.

---

## `FabricatorApi` Interface (`provides`)

```typescript
interface FabricatorApi {
  /**
   * Look up an engine design by ID.
   * Returns the design if registered, undefined otherwise.
   */
  getEngineDesign(id: string): EngineDesign | undefined
}
```

---

## Configuration

None. No `guild.json` entry needed.

---

## Implementation Notes

- The implementation is small: a `Map<string, EngineDesign>` populated by scanning kit contributions at startup, and a single `get` method. Ship it as a standalone package (`@shardworks/fabricator-apparatus`) to establish the dependency boundary and to own the `EngineDesign` type exports.
- The Instrumentarium's kit-scanning lifecycle is the model to follow — reactive consumption of `plugin:initialized` events, collecting contributions into an internal registry.

---

## Future

The Fabricator is deliberately thin. Its planned evolution:

**Capability resolution.** When dynamic rig extension arrives, the API grows:

```typescript
interface FabricatorApi {
  // ... existing methods ...

  /**
   * Resolve a declared need to an engine chain.
   * Searches installed engine designs for those that satisfy the need,
   * composes them into an ordered chain, and returns the chain for the
   * Spider to graft onto the rig.
   */
  resolve(need: string, context?: ResolutionContext): EngineChain | null
}
```

43	/**
44	 * An engine design — the unit of work the Fabricator catalogues and the
45	 * Spider executes. Kit authors import this type from @shardworks/fabricator-apparatus.
46	 */
47	export interface EngineDesign {
48	  /** Unique identifier for this engine design (e.g. 'draft', 'implement', 'review'). */
49	  id: string;
50	
51	  /**
52	   * Execute this engine.
53	   *
54	   * @param givens   — the engine's declared inputs, assembled by the Spider.
55	   * @param context  — minimal execution context: engine id and upstream yields.
56	   */
57	  run(givens: Record<string, unknown>, context: EngineRunContext): Promise<EngineRunResult>;
58	
59	  /**
60	   * Assemble yields from a completed session.
61	   *
62	   * Called by the Spider's collect step when a quick engine's session
63	   * reaches a terminal state. The engine looks up whatever it needs
64	   * via guild() — same dependency pattern as run().
65	   *
66	   * @param sessionId — the session to collect yields from (primary input).
67	   * @param givens    — same givens that were passed to run().
68	   * @param context   — same execution context that was passed to run().
69	   *
70	   * If not defined, the Spider uses a generic default:
71	   *   { sessionId, sessionStatus, output? }
72	   *
73	   * Only relevant for quick engines (those that return { status: 'launched' }).
74	   * Clockwork engines return yields directly from run().
75	   */
76	  collect?(sessionId: string, givens: Record<string, unknown>, context: EngineRunContext): Promise<unknown>;
77	}

Now I have a thorough understanding of the entire codebase. Let me now write the inventory document. Key findings:

## Architecture Summary

1. **Spider** (`packages/plugins/spider/`) is the main apparatus to modify
   - `spider.ts` - core logic with `crawl()`, `tryCollect()`, `tryRun()`, `trySpawn()`
   - `types.ts` - public types: `EngineStatus`, `EngineInstance`, `RigDoc`, `RigStatus`, `CrawlResult`, `SpiderApi`, `SpiderConfig`
   - `spider.test.ts` - comprehensive tests

2. The system uses several status enums: engines can be `'pending' | 'running' | 'completed' | 'failed' | 'cancelled'`, rigs are `'running' | 'completed' | 'failed'`, crawl results track `engine-completed | engine-started | rig-spawned | rig-completed`, and engine run results are either `completed` or `launched`. 3. The **SpiderApi** exposes `crawl()`, `show()`, `list()`, and `forWrit()` methods for interacting with the system.
4. The **Fabricator** manages `EngineDesign` objects by ID and will need to handle a new `BlockTypeDesign` concept.
5. Kit contributions currently flow through `engines`, `tools`, and `books` fields, but we need to add a `blockTypes` contribution field.

Now I'm looking at what needs to be added to support blocking behavior. The `EngineRunResult` needs a `blocked` status with `blockType` and `condition` fields, and both `EngineStatus` and `RigStatus` enums need `'blocked'` added. The `EngineInstance` will need new fields to track blocking state like `blockType`, `blockCondition`, `blockedAt`, and a `block` method. I'm also defining a new `BlockTypeDesign` interface in the fabricator with an `id`, a `checker` function that validates conditions asynchronously, a `conditionSchema`, and an optional `pollIntervalMs` for checking intervals. There's also a new `BlockRecord` type coming in the spider types.

The crawl logic needs several updates: a new Phase 4 to check blocked engines, modifications to `findRunnableEngine` to skip blocked ones, and CDC handler changes to handle `blocked` rig status without transitioning writes to failed. The tools need updates too—`rig-list` gets `'blocked'` in its status enum, `rig-show` can surface block metadata, and there's a new `rig-resume` tool for manually clearing blocks. The `SpiderApi` gets a `clearBlock` method, and the Fabricator registers `BlockTypeDesign` objects alongside engines.

I'm also noticing some documentation inconsistencies to clarify: the `building-engines.md` guide describes Clockworks handlers triggered by standing orders, which is a completely different system from the Fabricator `EngineDesign` engines used in Spider, even though they share the name. And the spider docs list only `crawlOneTool` and `crawlContinualTool`, but the actual code also registers `rigShowTool`, `rigListTool`, and `rigForWritTool`.

Now I need to add blocking support across both systems. I'm introducing an `EngineRunResult` variant for blocked states and a `BlockTypeDesign` type to the Fabricator, along with a `BlockTypeRegistry` and getter method. The Spider types need to expand to include 'blocked' statuses for both engines and rigs, plus new `BlockRecord` and `CrawlResult` variants, and a `clearBlock` method on the API. Then I'm updating the Spider implementation to check for blocked states, handle blocked results in the run loop, filter out blocked engines from the runnable set, and process blocked rig updates from the CDC handler.

I'm also updating the rig-list and rig-show tools to surface blocking information, creating a new rig-resume tool, and adding test coverage for the blocked lifecycle. The key architectural piece is that block types register as guild apparatus contributions, similar to how engines and tools work. a new step to check blocked engines. I'll add `tryCheckBlocked()` to iterate through blocked engines and run their checker function—if it returns true, the engine transitions back to pending so `tryRun()` can pick it up again. Since the brief says the Spider restarts the engine when it detects a change, it needs to re-run from the beginning, though the context should carry over prior block information. For communication, `EngineRunResult` needs a new blocked variant with the block type and condition, and `EngineRunContext` should include that prior blocking data when the engine restarts.

I'm also tracking the block state on the `EngineInstance` itself with a block object containing the type, condition, and timestamp, which gets cleared once the engine is unblocked and re-queued. Now I'll pull all of this together into the inventory document.

The inventory has been written to `/workspace/nexus-mk2/specs/implement-ability-to-pause-rigs/inventory.md`.

Here's a summary of what I found and catalogued:

---

## What I read

**Core Spider apparatus** (`packages/plugins/spider/`): the `types.ts` (all public types), `spider.ts` (full crawl logic — collect → run → spawn phases), all 5 engine implementations (draft, implement, review, revise, seal), all 5 tools (crawl-one, crawl-continual, rig-show, rig-list, rig-for-writ), and the test file (partial — 500+ lines).

**Fabricator** (`packages/plugins/fabricator/src/fabricator.ts`): `EngineDesign`, `EngineRunResult`, `EngineRunContext`, `FabricatorApi`, `EngineRegistry` — the extension point where block types must be added.

**Adjacent apparatus**: Stacks types (CDC, Book handles, transaction model), Clerk types (WritDoc, ClerkApi), core plugin types (Kit, Apparatus, StartupContext), Guild singleton.

**Architecture docs**: `spider.md`, `fabricator.md`, `rigging.md`, `stacks.md`, `clockworks.md`.

---

## Key findings for downstream analysis

1. **Two files own the design boundary**: `fabricator.ts` (adds `BlockTypeDesign` + `blocked` variant to `EngineRunResult`) and `spider/types.ts` (adds `blocked` to `EngineStatus`/`RigStatus`, new `BlockRecord`, new `CrawlResult` variants, extends `EngineRunContext`).

2. **Spider crawl loop gets a new phase**: `tryCheckBlocked()` runs blocked engine checkers via Fabricator on each crawl cycle, transitioning cleared engines back to `pending`.

3. **Critical rig-blocked logic**: A rig enters `blocked` status when no engines are `running` or `pending`-with-complete-upstream, but at least one is `blocked`. This is checked after an engine returns a blocked result.

4. **CDC handler must be guarded**: The existing handler that transitions writs on rig terminal state must NOT fire for `blocked` — only `completed` and `failed` remain terminal.

5. **Doc/code discrepancy**: `building-engines.md` describes a completely different "engine" concept (Clockworks standing-order handlers) that doesn't exist in the current apparatus codebase. Significant terminology collision.

content:
-
MODE: ANALYST

Here is the brief:

# Implement ability to block engines on external conditions

## Problem

Currently, once a rig is created the Spider starts each engine as it becomes ready, and engines can only "succeed" (with yields) or "fail" (causing the whole rig to fail). There is no way for an engine to signal that it has encountered an external condition it cannot resolve itself and must wait for.

## Desired behavior

Engines should be able to enter a **blocked** state — signaling to the Spider that the engine cannot proceed, but has not failed. The engine terminates cleanly and declares *what* it is waiting for using a structured block record. When the blocking condition clears, the Spider detects the change and restarts the engine, which may then run to completion, fail, or block again.

"Blocked" is the correct term: the engine wants to proceed but can't until a condition is met..

## Example blocking conditions (not exhaustive)

- Feedback needed from a human operator (e.g., a Stacks Book is written to)
- Waiting for an external system (e.g., a GitHub Actions workflow completes, a Vercel deployment finishes)
- Waiting for a time-based condition (e.g., entering a deployment window)
- Waiting for an internal dependency (e.g., another writ reaches a target status)

## Architecture: Block types and condition checkers

The key architectural decision: **engines declare what they're waiting for, but do not implement the checking logic.** Checking whether a block condition has cleared is handled by lightweight, non-LLM condition checkers registered as guild apparatus.

### Why this approach

The naive alternative — Spider polls blocked engines by restarting them, and engines self-check their conditions — is expensive. Most block checks are trivially answerable ("has this book been updated?" "is this workflow done?" "is it after 2pm?") but if the engine protocol requires spinning up a full agent session to check, you burn LLM tokens on what should be a `curl` call. Separating the checking concern from the engine also means common block types (GitHub workflow, scheduled time, book update) are write-once/use-everywhere rather than reimplemented in every engine.

### Block types as registered components

Block types are registered with the guild by apparatus, similar to engines and tools. A block type registration includes:

- A **checker** — a lightweight function/script that evaluates whether the condition has cleared. Signature: `(condition) => boolean`. No LLM involved.
- A **condition schema** — describes the shape of the condition payload the engine must provide. Enables validation at block time.
- A suggested **poll interval** — hint to the Spider for how frequently to check this type (don't hammer GitHub every 2 seconds; check timestamps every 30 seconds).

### Block records

When an engine exits blocked, it provides a block type and a structured condition payload. The Spider validates the block type exists in the guild and the condition matches the type's schema. If validation fails, the engine is failed immediately (fail-fast — catches typos, missing registrations, engines assuming apparatus that isn't installed).

Valid block records are persisted and the engine enters the `blocked` status.

### Condition checking: Spider-owned

The Spider owns the block-checking loop as a natural extension of its rig-tending responsibilities. On each crawl cycle, for each blocked engine, the Spider runs the registered checker for that block's type. If the checker returns true, the block is cleared and the engine is transitioned back to a runnable state.

For internal conditions (book updates, writ status changes), the system can use CDC/events to clear blocks immediately rather than waiting for the next poll tick. But polling is the baseline mechanism — events are an optimization layered on top.

### Engine author experience

An engine author's responsibility is minimal:

1. **Detect** the blocking condition during engine execution
2. **Declare** what they're waiting for: a block type string and a condition payload matching that type's schema
3. That's it. No check logic. No re-check on startup.

When the engine is restarted after unblocking, it receives context about the prior block (so it can pick up where it left off if needed).

### Checker author experience

A block type author writes:

1. A checker function: `(condition: T) => Promise<boolean>` — returns true if the condition has cleared
2. A condition schema for validation
3. Registers it as guild apparatus

Built-in block types should ship with the framework for common cases: book updates, writ status, scheduled time, and at least one external-system example.

## Rig-level blocked status

When all forward progress in a rig is blocked (the only non-completed engines are blocked, plus their pending dependents), the rig itself transitions to `blocked` status. This gives operators clear queryability ("show me all blocked rigs") and prevents the crawl loop from wasting cycles on rigs that can't make progress. The rig transitions back to `running` when any engine is unblocked.

## Operator surface

- `rig-list` should support filtering by `blocked` status
- `rig-show` should surface block metadata (what the engine is waiting for, how long it's been blocked)
- A `rig-resume` tool provides manual override — an operator can clear a block regardless of the checker, for any block type

## CrawlResult observability

The crawl loop should emit result variants for block and unblock events at both engine and rig level, so callers of `crawl()` (including `crawl-continual`) can observe these state transitions.

MODE: ANALYST

Here is the brief:

# Implement ability to block engines on external conditions

## Problem

Currently, once a rig is created the Spider starts each engine as it becomes ready, and engines can only "succeed" (with yields) or "fail" (causing the whole rig to fail). There is no way for an engine to signal that it has encountered an external condition it cannot resolve itself and must wait for.

## Desired behavior

Engines should be able to enter a **blocked** state — signaling to the Spider that the engine cannot proceed, but has not failed. The engine terminates cleanly and declares *what* it is waiting for using a structured block record. When the blocking condition clears, the Spider detects the change and restarts the engine, which may then run to completion, fail, or block again.

"Blocked" is the correct term: the engine wants to proceed but can't until a condition is met..

## Example blocking conditions (not exhaustive)

- Feedback needed from a human operator (e.g., a Stacks Book is written to)
- Waiting for an external system (e.g., a GitHub Actions workflow completes, a Vercel deployment finishes)
- Waiting for a time-based condition (e.g., entering a deployment window)
- Waiting for an internal dependency (e.g., another writ reaches a target status)

## Architecture: Block types and condition checkers

The key architectural decision: **engines declare what they're waiting for, but do not implement the checking logic.** Checking whether a block condition has cleared is handled by lightweight, non-LLM condition checkers registered as guild apparatus.

### Why this approach

The naive alternative — Spider polls blocked engines by restarting them, and engines self-check their conditions — is expensive. Most block checks are trivially answerable ("has this book been updated?" "is this workflow done?" "is it after 2pm?") but if the engine protocol requires spinning up a full agent session to check, you burn LLM tokens on what should be a `curl` call. Separating the checking concern from the engine also means common block types (GitHub workflow, scheduled time, book update) are write-once/use-everywhere rather than reimplemented in every engine.

### Block types as registered components

Block types are registered with the guild by apparatus, similar to engines and tools. A block type registration includes:

- A **checker** — a lightweight function/script that evaluates whether the condition has cleared. Signature: `(condition) => boolean`. No LLM involved.
- A **condition schema** — describes the shape of the condition payload the engine must provide. Enables validation at block time.
- A suggested **poll interval** — hint to the Spider for how frequently to check this type (don't hammer GitHub every 2 seconds; check timestamps every 30 seconds).

### Block records

When an engine exits blocked, it provides a block type and a structured condition payload. The Spider validates the block type exists in the guild and the condition matches the type's schema. If validation fails, the engine is failed immediately (fail-fast — catches typos, missing registrations, engines assuming apparatus that isn't installed).

Valid block records are persisted and the engine enters the `blocked` status.

### Condition checking: Spider-owned

The Spider owns the block-checking loop as a natural extension of its rig-tending responsibilities. On each crawl cycle, for each blocked engine, the Spider runs the registered checker for that block's type. If the checker returns true, the block is cleared and the engine is transitioned back to a runnable state.

For internal conditions (book updates, writ status changes), the system can use CDC/events to clear blocks immediately rather than waiting for the next poll tick. But polling is the baseline mechanism — events are an optimization layered on top.

### Engine author experience

An engine author's responsibility is minimal:

1. **Detect** the blocking condition during engine execution
2. **Declare** what they're waiting for: a block type string and a condition payload matching that type's schema
3. That's it. No check logic. No re-check on startup.

When the engine is restarted after unblocking, it receives context about the prior block (so it can pick up where it left off if needed).

### Checker author experience

A block type author writes:

1. A checker function: `(condition: T) => Promise<boolean>` — returns true if the condition has cleared
2. A condition schema for validation
3. Registers it as guild apparatus

Built-in block types should ship with the framework for common cases: book updates, writ status, scheduled time, and at least one external-system example.

## Rig-level blocked status

When all forward progress in a rig is blocked (the only non-completed engines are blocked, plus their pending dependents), the rig itself transitions to `blocked` status. This gives operators clear queryability ("show me all blocked rigs") and prevents the crawl loop from wasting cycles on rigs that can't make progress. The rig transitions back to `running` when any engine is unblocked.

## Operator surface

- `rig-list` should support filtering by `blocked` status
- `rig-show` should surface block metadata (what the engine is waiting for, how long it's been blocked)
- A `rig-resume` tool provides manual override — an operator can clear a block regardless of the checker, for any block type

## CrawlResult observability

The crawl loop should emit result variants for block and unblock events at both engine and rig level, so callers of `crawl()` (including `crawl-continual`) can observe these state transitions.

38	- `tryRun()`: handle `{ status: 'blocked' }` result — validate block type, store block record, set engine to `blocked` status; also update rig status to `blocked` if all forward progress is blocked
39	- `findRunnableEngine()`: must skip `blocked` engines (they are not `pending` in the ordinary sense until unblocked)
40	- `buildUpstreamMap()`: no change needed (blocked engines have no yields yet)
41	- `failEngine()`: currently cancels `pending` engines — must also cancel engines that were `blocked` (since they won't unblock if the rig fails)

56	Extensive test file (23K tokens). Must add test coverage for:
57	- Engine returning `blocked` result → engine enters `blocked` status
58	- Block type validation failure → immediate engine failure
59	- Blocked engine's block record stored on `EngineInstance`

62	- `tryCheckBlocked()` with checker returning false → engine stays blocked
