79	- `packages/plugins/spider/src/engines/revise.ts` — no change
80	- `packages/plugins/spider/src/engines/index.ts` — no change
81	- `packages/plugins/fabricator/src/fabricator.test.ts` — may need new tests
82	- `packages/plugins/clerk/src/types.ts` — no change (writ statuses unaffected)
83	
84	---
85	
86	## Current Type Signatures (Verbatim)
87	
88	### `packages/plugins/spider/src/types.ts`
89	
90	```typescript
91	export type EngineStatus = 'pending' | 'running' | 'completed' | 'failed' | 'cancelled';
92	
93	export interface EngineInstance {
94	  id: string;
95	  designId: string;
96	  status: EngineStatus;
97	  upstream: string[];
98	  givensSpec: Record<string, unknown>;
99	  yields?: unknown;
100	  error?: string;
101	  sessionId?: string;
102	  startedAt?: string;
103	  completedAt?: string;
104	}
105	
106	export type RigStatus = 'running' | 'completed' | 'failed';
107	
108	export interface RigDoc {
109	  [key: string]: unknown;
110	  id: string;
111	  writId: string;
112	  status: RigStatus;
113	  engines: EngineInstance[];
114	  createdAt: string;
115	}
116	
117	export interface RigFilters {
118	  status?: RigStatus;
119	  limit?: number;
120	  offset?: number;
121	}
122	
123	export type CrawlResult =
124	  | { action: 'engine-completed'; rigId: string; engineId: string }
125	  | { action: 'engine-started'; rigId: string; engineId: string }
126	  | { action: 'rig-spawned'; rigId: string; writId: string }
127	  | { action: 'rig-completed'; rigId: string; writId: string; outcome: 'completed' | 'failed' };
128	
129	export interface SpiderApi {
130	  crawl(): Promise<CrawlResult | null>;
131	  show(id: string): Promise<RigDoc>;
132	  list(filters?: RigFilters): Promise<RigDoc[]>;
133	  forWrit(writId: string): Promise<RigDoc | null>;
134	}
135	
136	export interface SpiderConfig {
137	  role?: string;
138	  pollIntervalMs?: number;
139	  buildCommand?: string;
140	  testCommand?: string;
141	}
142	```
143	
144	### `packages/plugins/fabricator/src/fabricator.ts`
145	
146	```typescript
147	export interface EngineRunContext {
148	  engineId: string;
149	  upstream: Record<string, unknown>;
150	}
151	
152	export type EngineRunResult =
153	  | { status: 'completed'; yields: unknown }
154	  | { status: 'launched'; sessionId: string };
155	
156	export interface EngineDesign {
157	  id: string;
158	  run(givens: Record<string, unknown>, context: EngineRunContext): Promise<EngineRunResult>;
159	  collect?(sessionId: string, givens: Record<string, unknown>, context: EngineRunContext): Promise<unknown>;
160	}
161	
162	export interface FabricatorApi {
163	  getEngineDesign(id: string): EngineDesign | undefined;
164	}
165	```
166	
167	---
168	
169	## Spider Core Logic (Key Functions)
170	
171	### `findRunnableEngine(rig: RigDoc): EngineInstance | null`
172	```typescript
173	function findRunnableEngine(rig: RigDoc): EngineInstance | null {
174	  for (const engine of rig.engines) {
175	    if (engine.status !== 'pending') continue;
176	    const allUpstreamDone = engine.upstream.every((upstreamId) => {
177	      const dep = rig.engines.find((e) => e.id === upstreamId);
178	      return dep?.status === 'completed';
179	    });
180	    if (allUpstreamDone) return engine;
181	  }
182	  return null;
183	}
184	```
185	This function only checks `pending` engines. After adding `blocked`, `blocked` engines must NOT appear here.
186	
187	### `failEngine(rig, engineId, errorMessage)`
188	Currently cancels all `pending` engines and fails the rig. After adding `blocked`, `blocked` engines should also be cancelled when the rig fails.
189	
190	### `tryRun()` — handling engine run results
191	```typescript
192	engineResult = await design.run(givens, context);
193	
194	if (engineResult.status === 'launched') {
195	  // store sessionId, mark engine 'running'
196	  return { action: 'engine-started', ... };
197	}
198	
199	// 'completed' case — store yields, advance engine
200	```
201	Must add a `'blocked'` branch here.
202	
203	### `tryCollect()` — Phase 1 priority
204	Iterates `running` rigs, finds engine with `status === 'running' && sessionId`. Checks session terminal state.  
205	No structural change needed for the blocked path — a blocked engine has `status === 'blocked'`, not `running`, so `tryCollect()` won't touch it.
206	
207	### `crawl()` call order
208	```typescript
209	const collected = await tryCollect();  // Phase 1
210	if (collected) return collected;
211	
212	const ran = await tryRun();            // Phase 2
213	if (ran) return ran;
214	
215	const spawned = await trySpawn();      // Phase 3
216	if (spawned) return spawned;
217	
218	return null;
219	```
220	New `tryCheckBlocked()` phase needs to be inserted. Priority question: before or after collect/run? The brief says checking blocks is polling-based and happens on each crawl cycle. Most sensible: run it before or after collect but before run/spawn — blocked checks unblock engines, which then become runnable.
221	
222	### CDC handler (rigs book Phase 1)
223	```typescript
224	if (rig.status === 'completed') { await clerk.transition(..., 'completed', ...) }
225	else if (rig.status === 'failed') { await clerk.transition(..., 'failed', ...) }
226	```
227	After adding `blocked`, the handler must NOT fire for `blocked` status — only `completed` and `failed` are terminal.
228	
229	---
230	
231	## Fabricator Engine Registry (Relevant Internals)
232	
233	```typescript
234	class EngineRegistry {
235	  private readonly designs = new Map<string, EngineDesign>();
236	  register(plugin: LoadedPlugin): void { ... }  // scans plugin.apparatus.supportKit.engines
237	  private registerFromKit(kit: Record<string, unknown>): void { ... }
238	  get(id: string): EngineDesign | undefined { ... }
239	}
240	```
241	
242	A parallel `BlockTypeRegistry` class would follow the same pattern, scanning `blockTypes` from kit/supportKit contributions. The Fabricator's `start()` already handles both initial kit scan and `plugin:initialized` events — block type scanning would piggyback the same hooks.
243	
244	```typescript
245	// isEngineDesign type guard
246	function isEngineDesign(value: unknown): value is EngineDesign {
247	  return typeof value === 'object' && value !== null &&
248	    typeof (value as Record<string, unknown>).id === 'string' &&
249	    typeof (value as Record<string, unknown>).run === 'function';
250	}
251	```
252	A parallel `isBlockTypeDesign()` guard needed.
253	
254	---
255	
256	## Tool Patterns
257	
258	All existing tools follow this pattern:
259	```typescript
260	export default tool({
261	  name: 'tool-name',
262	  description: '...',
263	  instructions: '...',
264	  params: { ... },
265	  permission: 'read' | 'spider:write',
266	  handler: async (params) => {
267	    const spider = guild().apparatus<SpiderApi>('spider');
268	    return spider.method(...);
269	  },
270	});
271	```
272	
273	The `rig-resume` tool would follow this exact pattern, calling `spider.resume(rigId, engineId)`. Permission should be `'spider:write'` (same as crawl tools — it mutates state).
274	
275	---
276	
277	## Stacks Book: `spider/rigs`
278	
279	Current indexes: `['status', 'writId', ['status', 'writId'], 'createdAt']`
280	
281	After adding `blocked` status to `RigStatus`, the query in `tryCheckBlocked()` would query `{ where: [['status', '=', 'blocked']] }`. The existing `status` index covers this. No schema change needed.
282	
283	Query in `tryRun()` and `tryCollect()` both filter `where: [['status', '=', 'running']]` — these don't change.
284	
285	---
286	
287	## Adjacent Patterns
288	
289	### How the existing `cancelled` status propagates
290	When `failEngine()` is called, pending downstream engines are marked `cancelled`. The same logic applies to blocked engines when a rig fails — blocked engines should also be cancelled, not left dangling.
291	
292	### How `EngineStatus` is used in `buildUpstreamMap()`
293	```typescript
294	if (engine.status === 'completed' && engine.yields !== undefined) {
295	  upstream[engine.id] = engine.yields;
296	}
297	```
298	Blocked engines never reach completed, so they contribute nothing to upstream. No change needed here.
299	
300	### How `allCompleted` check works in `tryRun()`
301	```typescript
302	const allCompleted = completedEngines.every((e) => e.status === 'completed');
303	```
304	After adding blocked/cancelled: `allCompleted` already returns false if any engine is `pending`, `running`, `cancelled`, or (new) `blocked`. The rig-completed logic is unaffected. BUT: the rig-blocked determination requires a different check — "all non-completed engines are either blocked or pending-with-blocked-upstream."
305	
306	### How `rig-completed` is returned
307	Both `tryCollect()` and `tryRun()` check `allCompleted` and return `rig-completed` with `outcome: 'completed'`. The rig-blocked case needs a new check after an engine transitions to blocked.
308	
309	---
310	
311	## Rig-Level Blocked Logic
312	
313	Determining when a rig is blocked (all forward progress blocked):
314	
315	```
316	rig.blocked iff:
317	  - no engine has status 'running'
318	  - no engine has status 'pending' with all upstream completed
319	  - at least one engine has status 'blocked'
320	```
321	
322	In other words: there are no runnable engines and no running engines, but there are blocked engines preventing completion. This check runs inside `tryRun()` when a new blocked engine causes the rig to have no more runnable work.
323	
324	Similarly, `rig.running` resumes when any engine is unblocked (returned to `pending`).
325	
326	---
327	
328	## CrawlResult Priority Question
329	
330	The brief says the crawl loop should emit block/unblock variants. Current priority: collect > run > spawn. Where does `tryCheckBlocked()` fit?
331	
332	Options:
333	1. **Before collect** — unblocked engines become runnable immediately, but delays collecting completed sessions
334	2. **After collect, before run** — unblocked engines become runnable in same cycle their block clears; collect still prioritized
335	3. **After run, before spawn** — simplest: block checking is low-priority, done when nothing else to run
336	
337	The brief says checking is "as a natural extension of its rig-tending responsibilities" — suggests it runs as part of the crawl loop at whatever priority makes sense. Option 2 (after collect, before run) seems most natural: the blocked check unblocks an engine, then run picks it up in the *next* crawl. This matches the "one unit of work per crawl" model.
338	
339	---
340	
341	## Test File Analysis
342	
343	`packages/plugins/spider/src/spider.test.ts` (~500+ lines):
344	- Uses `buildFixture()` which wires in-memory Stacks + mock Animator + Clerk + Fabricator + Spider
345	- Custom engine designs are injected via `fire('plugin:initialized', fakePlugin)` pattern
346	- Mock Animator writes session records immediately to the sessions book
347	- Tests use `setSessionOutcome()` to control what session results the mock produces
348	- Engine designs contributed via `apparatus.supportKit.engines`
349	- Pattern for test engines:
350	  ```typescript
351	  const badEngine: EngineDesign = {
352	    id: 'bad-engine',
353	    async run() { return { status: 'completed', yields: { fn: (() => {}) as any } }; },
354	  };
355	  ```
356	- For blocked engine tests, would need an engine that returns `{ status: 'blocked', blockType: 'test-condition', condition: { ... } }` and a registered block type with a controllable checker.
357	
358	---
359	
360	## Built-in Block Types (per brief)
361	
362	The brief says: "Built-in block types should ship with the framework for common cases: book updates, writ status, scheduled time, and at least one external-system example."
363	
364	These would be contributed by the Spider's support kit (or a new `block-types` kit) and registered via the `blockTypes` kit field. Each is a `BlockTypeDesign`:
365	
366	1. **`book-updated`** — checker watches for changes to a specific book/document
367	2. **`writ-status`** — checker polls for a writ reaching a target status
368	3. **`scheduled-time`** — checker evaluates `Date.now() >= condition.resumeAt`
369	4. **`github-workflow`** — calls GitHub API to check workflow run status (example external system)
370	
371	Where to contribute these is a design decision (in Spider's support kit? separate package?). The brief doesn't specify a package boundary. The Spider support kit is the natural home for built-in types since the Spider already contributes engines there.
372	
373	---
374	
375	## `EngineRunContext` Extension
376	
377	When an engine is restarted after being unblocked, it needs context about the prior block. The current `EngineRunContext`:
378	```typescript
379	export interface EngineRunContext {
380	  engineId: string;
381	  upstream: Record<string, unknown>;
382	}
383	```
384	Must add `priorBlock?: BlockRecord` — populated by the Spider when an engine restarts after a block. The Spider reads this from the engine's `block` field before clearing it.
385	
386	---
387	
388	## `SpiderApi.resume()` Shape
389	
390	The brief calls it `rig-resume` (the tool name) with "manual override — an operator can clear a block regardless of the checker, for any block type."
391	
392	The API method could be:
393	```typescript
394	resume(rigId: string, engineId: string): Promise<void>
395	```
396	This would transition the engine from `blocked` back to `pending`. The rig would also transition back to `running` (since it now has a runnable engine).
397	
398	---
399	
400	## CDC / Event-Driven Unblocking (Optional Optimization)
401	
402	The brief mentions: "For internal conditions (book updates, writ status changes), the system can use CDC/events to clear blocks immediately rather than waiting for the next poll tick. But polling is the baseline mechanism — events are an optimization layered on top."
403	
404	This suggests: Phase 1 is polling-based (every crawl cycle). An optional Phase 2 might have specific block type implementations register CDC watchers on the Stacks that proactively trigger re-checking when a relevant book changes. This is an optimization not a requirement for MVP.
405	
406	The inventory notes this exists as a described future optimization but is out of scope for the initial implementation.
407	
408	---
409	
410	## Doc/Code Discrepancies
411	
412	1. **`docs/architecture/apparatus/spider.md` tool list:** Docs say `supportKit.tools: [crawlOneTool, crawlContinualTool]` but actual code includes `[crawlOneTool, crawlContinualTool, rigShowTool, rigListTool, rigForWritTool]` — three additional tools undocumented.
413	
414	2. **`docs/guides/building-engines.md`:** This guide describes a completely different engine concept — Clockworks handlers registered via `nexus-engine.json` and standing orders. This is NOT the same as `EngineDesign` objects registered with the Fabricator. The terminology collision is significant. The guide appears to document a pre-apparatus architecture or a parallel system not reflected in the current `spider`/`fabricator` apparatus code. No Clockworks apparatus or `clockworks.ts` file exists in the current codebase's packages.
415	
416	3. **`docs/architecture/apparatus/spider.md` static graph:** Docs describe `yields: null` as initial value on EngineInstances, but actual code uses `yields?: unknown` (optional field, not set at spawn time).
417	
418	4. **`docs/architecture/apparatus/spider.md` CDC handler resolution:** Doc shows `sealYields.sealedCommit` etc. in resolution string, but actual code uses `JSON.stringify(sealEngine.yields)` — less structured.
419	
420	---
421	
422	## Package Dependency Graph
423	
424	```
425	@shardworks/spider-apparatus
426	  → @shardworks/fabricator-apparatus  (engine design lookup)
427	  → @shardworks/stacks-apparatus      (rigs book, sessions read, writs read, CDC)
428	  → @shardworks/clerk-apparatus       (writ transitions)
429	  → @shardworks/animator-apparatus    (session docs type)
430	  → @shardworks/tools-apparatus       (tool() factory)
431	  → @shardworks/codexes-apparatus     (draft/seal engines use this)
432	  → @shardworks/nexus-core            (guild(), generateId, etc.)
433	
434	@shardworks/fabricator-apparatus
435	  → @shardworks/nexus-core            (LoadedPlugin, guild, etc.)
436	```
437	
438	Block type checkers (added to Fabricator) have no new package dependencies — they run within the Fabricator's already-loaded context. Individual block type implementations may need apparatus dependencies (e.g., the `book-updated` checker would use Stacks, `github-workflow` would use `node:https`), accessed via `guild()` in the checker function body.
439	
440	---
441	
442	## Existing Tests Structure
443	
444	`packages/plugins/spider/src/spider.test.ts` test sections:
445	- "Fabricator — Spider engine registration"
446	- "walk() — idle"
447	- "walk() — spawn"
448	- "walk() — priority ordering: collect > run > spawn"
449	- "engine readiness — upstream must complete first"
450	- "implement engine execution"
451	- "yield serialization failure"
452	- "implement engine — Animator integration"
453	- "quick engine — collect"
454	- (more sections likely below line 750, not read)
455	
456	New test sections needed:
457	- "blocked engine — engine returns blocked result"
458	- "blocked engine — block type validation"
459	- "blocked engine — rig transitions to blocked"
460	- "blocked engine — condition checker clears block"
461	- "blocked engine — rig resumes when unblocked"
462	- "blocked engine — resume() tool clears block manually"
463	- "blocked engine — prior block context on restart"
464	- "blocked engine — failure cancels blocked engines"
465	
466	`packages/plugins/fabricator/src/fabricator.test.ts` — not read but exists; would need block type design tests.
467

content.text: Now let me re-read some key files for precision on specific decision points:

Good. Now I have a thorough picture. Let me also verify the `tryRun` rig query — it only queries `status === 'running'` rigs, so blocked rigs won't be checked there. The `tryCheckBlocked` step needs to query both `running` and `blocked` rigs (or just `blocked` rigs).

Good — the CDC handler already only fires on `completed` and `failed`, so `blocked` transitions are naturally ignored. No change needed to guard logic; the existing if/else-if structure handles it.

Now let me write the three output files:

input.content:
brief: |
  Engines should be able to enter a **blocked** state — signaling to the Spider that the engine cannot proceed, but has not failed. The engine terminates cleanly and declares *what* it is waiting for using a structured block record. When the blocking condition clears, the Spider detects the change and restarts the engine, which may then run to completion, fail, or block again. Block types are registered components with lightweight checkers. The Spider owns the block-checking loop. Rigs themselves transition to blocked when all forward progress is blocked. Operator surface includes rig-list filtering, rig-show metadata, and a rig-resume manual override tool. CrawlResult should emit block/unblock variants.
slug: implement-ability-to-pause-rigs

scope:
  - id: S1
    description: "Engine blocked status — engines can return a 'blocked' result from run(), the Spider validates and persists the block record, and the engine enters a blocked status"
    rationale: "Core primitive. Everything else depends on engines being able to signal a blocked state."
    included: true

  - id: S2
    description: "Block type registry — BlockTypeDesign interface in the Fabricator, kit contribution scanning for blockTypes field, FabricatorApi.getBlockTypeDesign() lookup method"
    rationale: "Brief explicitly says block types are registered components with checkers, condition schemas, and poll intervals, analogous to engine designs."
    included: true

  - id: S3
    description: "Spider block-checking loop — new tryCheckBlocked() crawl phase that iterates blocked engines, runs registered checkers, and transitions cleared engines back to pending"
    rationale: "Brief says the Spider owns the block-checking loop and checks on each crawl cycle."
    included: true

  - id: S4
    description: "Rig-level blocked status — rig transitions to blocked when all forward progress is blocked, back to running when any engine is unblocked"
    rationale: "Brief explicitly calls for rig-level blocked status for operator queryability and to avoid wasting crawl cycles."
    included: true

  - id: S5
    description: "CrawlResult observability — new engine-blocked, engine-unblocked, rig-blocked variants emitted by crawl()"
    rationale: "Brief explicitly says crawl loop should emit result variants for block and unblock events."
    included: true

  - id: S6
    description: "Operator surface — rig-list supports blocked filter, rig-show surfaces block metadata, new rig-resume tool for manual block clearing"
    rationale: "Brief explicitly lists these three operator surface requirements."
    included: true

  - id: S7
    description: "Prior block context — restarted engines receive context about their prior block via EngineRunContext.priorBlock"
    rationale: "Brief says 'when the engine is restarted after unblocking, it receives context about the prior block.'"
    included: true

  - id: S8
    description: "Built-in block types — framework ships common block types (book-updated, writ-status, scheduled-time, and at least one external example)"
    rationale: "Brief says 'built-in block types should ship with the framework for common cases.' This is separable — the mechanism works without built-ins."
    included: true

  - id: S9
    description: "Block condition schema validation — the Spider validates the engine's condition payload against the block type's declared condition schema at block time"
    rationale: "Brief says condition schema enables validation at block time and validation failure causes immediate engine failure (fail-fast)."
    included: true

input.content:
decisions:
  # ── S1: Engine blocked status ──────────────────────────────────────

  - id: D1
    scope: [S1]
    category: api
    observable: true
    question: "What shape does the blocked EngineRunResult variant take?"
    context: "EngineRunResult currently has two variants: { status: 'completed'; yields } and { status: 'launched'; sessionId }. The engine must declare a block type and condition payload."
    options:
      a: "{ status: 'blocked'; blockType: string; condition: unknown } — flat, parallel to existing variants"
      b: "{ status: 'blocked'; block: { type: string; condition: unknown } } — nested block record"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "Flat structure matches the existing variant style (status + payload fields). The Spider assembles the BlockRecord internally by adding blockedAt."

  - id: D2
    scope: [S1]
    category: api
    observable: true
    question: "What fields does the BlockRecord stored on EngineInstance contain?"
    context: "When an engine blocks, the Spider persists a block record on the EngineInstance. This record must contain enough for the checker to evaluate and for operator display."
    options:
      a: "{ type: string; condition: unknown; blockedAt: string } — minimal: block type, condition payload, timestamp"
      b: "{ type: string; condition: unknown; blockedAt: string; attempts: number } — adds a re-check counter"
      c: "{ type: string; condition: unknown; blockedAt: string; message?: string } — adds optional human-readable description from engine"
    selected: c
    analysis:
      recommendation: c
      confidence: medium
      rationale: "An optional message costs nothing but improves rig-show operator experience. The engine can explain what it's waiting for in human terms. Attempts tracking (b) can be added later if needed."

  - id: D3
    scope: [S1]
    category: implementation
    observable: false
    question: "When an engine returns blocked, does the Spider mark it running first then blocked, or directly pending→blocked?"
    context: "Currently tryRun() marks an engine 'running' before calling design.run(). If run() returns blocked, the engine was briefly 'running'. The Spider needs to then update to 'blocked'."
    options:
      a: "Keep existing pattern — mark running before run(), then update to blocked after. Two patches."
      b: "Keep existing pattern — mark running before run(), then update to blocked after. Single patch replaces 'running' with 'blocked'."
    selected: b
    analysis:
      recommendation: b
      confidence: high
      rationale: "The engine IS running while run() executes (it's synchronous from the Spider's perspective). When run() returns blocked, the Spider patches from running to blocked in one update. This is the same pattern as the launched/completed branches which also update after run()."

  - id: D4
    scope: [S1]
    category: implementation
    observable: false
    question: "Should failEngine() cancel blocked engines in addition to pending ones?"
    context: "failEngine() currently cancels all pending engines when one fails. Blocked engines are also non-terminal and can never complete if the rig fails."
    options:
      a: "Yes — cancel both pending and blocked engines on rig failure"
      b: "No — only cancel pending, leave blocked as-is"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "Blocked engines have no path to completion once the rig has failed. Leaving them blocked would be misleading. The failEngine() predicate should be e.status === 'pending' || e.status === 'blocked'."

  # ── S2: Block type registry ────────────────────────────────────────

  - id: D5
    scope: [S2]
    category: api
    observable: true
    question: "What is the BlockTypeDesign interface shape?"
    context: "The brief specifies a checker function, condition schema, and suggested poll interval. The checker signature is (condition) => Promise<boolean>. The schema needs to be machine-validatable."
    options:
      a: "{ id: string; check: (condition: unknown) => Promise<boolean>; conditionSchema: ZodSchema; pollIntervalMs?: number }"
      b: "{ id: string; check: (condition: unknown) => Promise<boolean>; conditionSchema?: ZodSchema; pollIntervalMs?: number } — schema optional"
      c: "{ id: string; check: (condition: unknown) => Promise<boolean>; validate?: (condition: unknown) => boolean; pollIntervalMs?: number } — custom validate function instead of Zod"
    selected: c
    analysis:
      recommendation: c
      confidence: high
      rationale: "The Fabricator doesn't depend on Zod today. Introducing a Zod dependency for condition schemas would break the clean dependency boundary. A validate function is more flexible — block type authors can use Zod internally if they want, or plain JS checks. The Spider calls validate(condition) at block time; if it returns false, the engine fails. If validate is not provided, any condition is accepted."

  - id: D6
    scope: [S2]
    category: api
    observable: true
    question: "Where does BlockTypeDesign live — Fabricator package or Spider package?"
    context: "EngineDesign lives in the Fabricator because the Fabricator is the capability catalog. Block types are also capabilities that kit authors register. But the Spider is the consumer. The Fabricator's role is to be the single registry for design-time artifacts."
    options:
      a: "Fabricator — BlockTypeDesign defined and registered in @shardworks/fabricator-apparatus, parallel to EngineDesign"
      b: "Spider — BlockTypeDesign defined and registered in @shardworks/spider-apparatus, since it's spider-specific"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "The Fabricator is the guild's capability catalog. Engine designs live there; block type designs are the same kind of thing — registered at startup, looked up at runtime. Kit authors import the type from the Fabricator. The Spider queries the Fabricator, same pattern as engine design lookup."

  - id: D7
    scope: [S2]
    category: implementation
    observable: false
    question: "How does the Fabricator scan blockTypes from kits?"
    context: "The Fabricator currently scans kit.engines using EngineRegistry.registerFromKit(). The same pattern works for blockTypes."
    options:
      a: "Add a parallel BlockTypeRegistry class with its own registerFromKit() scanning kit.blockTypes"
      b: "Extend EngineRegistry to handle both engines and blockTypes in a single class"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "Separate registries for separate concerns. The EngineRegistry and BlockTypeRegistry are small, parallel classes. Merging them would conflate two distinct type domains."

  - id: D8
    scope: [S2]
    category: api
    observable: true
    question: "What method does FabricatorApi expose for block type lookup?"
    context: "FabricatorApi currently has getEngineDesign(id). Need a parallel method for block types."
    options:
      a: "getBlockType(id: string): BlockTypeDesign | undefined"
      b: "getBlockTypeDesign(id: string): BlockTypeDesign | undefined — consistent naming with getEngineDesign"
    selected: b
    analysis:
      recommendation: b
      confidence: high
      rationale: "Consistent naming with getEngineDesign. Both return Design objects."

  - id: D9
    scope: [S2]
    category: api
    observable: true
    question: "What kit contribution field name is used for block type registrations?"
    context: "Engines use kit.engines. Tools use kit.tools. Need a field name for block type designs."
    options:
      a: "blockTypes — descriptive, clear"
      b: "blocks — shorter, but ambiguous (could mean blocked records)"
      c: "checkers — names the function, not the concept"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "blockTypes is unambiguous and parallel to the existing conventions. The Fabricator consumes: ['engines'] becomes consumes: ['engines', 'blockTypes']."

  # ── S3: Spider block-checking loop ─────────────────────────────────

  - id: D10
    scope: [S3]
    category: product
    observable: true
    question: "Where in the crawl priority order does tryCheckBlocked() run?"
    context: "Current order is collect > run > spawn. Each crawl() does one thing. The block check unblocks engines which then become runnable on a subsequent crawl."
    options:
      a: "collect > checkBlocked > run > spawn — block checks happen early, unblocked engines runnable next crawl"
      b: "collect > run > checkBlocked > spawn — block checks are lower priority than running new engines"
      c: "checkBlocked > collect > run > spawn — highest priority, unblocks as fast as possible"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "Collecting completed sessions should remain highest priority (it unblocks downstream engines). Block checking logically follows — it also unblocks engines. Running and spawning are lower priority. Option (a) gives unblocked engines a one-crawl delay before running, which is consistent with the one-action-per-crawl model."

  - id: D11
    scope: [S3]
    category: implementation
    observable: false
    question: "Does tryCheckBlocked() query running rigs, blocked rigs, or both?"
    context: "A rig with a blocked engine might still have status 'running' (other engines are still in progress). Or it might be 'blocked' (all progress halted). The checker needs to find blocked engines in both cases."
    options:
      a: "Query rigs with status IN ('running', 'blocked') and scan for blocked engines"
      b: "Query only blocked rigs — running rigs with blocked engines will eventually become blocked rigs when other engines complete"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "A running rig can have one blocked engine and one running engine. When the running engine completes, the blocked engine might be the only thing left. If we only check blocked rigs, the block check would be delayed until the rig transitions to blocked. Checking both ensures blocked engines are evaluated on every cycle regardless of rig status."

  - id: D12
    scope: [S3]
    category: implementation
    observable: false
    question: "When a checker throws an error, what happens to the blocked engine?"
    context: "Checkers are lightweight functions that may fail (network error, invalid condition, etc.). The brief doesn't specify error handling."
    options:
      a: "Log warning, skip — engine stays blocked. Retry next cycle."
      b: "Fail the engine — checker error is an unrecoverable configuration problem"
      c: "Log warning, skip, increment a retry counter — fail after N consecutive errors"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: medium
      rationale: "Checkers are external-facing (HTTP calls, file system checks). Transient failures are expected. Failing the engine on a network blip would be too aggressive. Logging and retrying next cycle is resilient. The operator can use rig-resume if the checker is permanently broken."

  - id: D13
    scope: [S3]
    category: implementation
    observable: false
    question: "Does tryCheckBlocked() respect the block type's suggested pollIntervalMs?"
    context: "The brief mentions a suggested poll interval per block type to avoid hammering external APIs. But the crawl loop already has its own pollIntervalMs (default 5000ms)."
    options:
      a: "Yes — track lastCheckedAt per blocked engine, skip if less than pollIntervalMs since last check"
      b: "No — check every cycle. The crawl loop's own interval is the throttle."
      c: "Yes — but store lastCheckedAt on the block record in the EngineInstance, persisted in Stacks"
    selected: c
    analysis:
      recommendation: c
      confidence: high
      rationale: "Without per-block-type throttling, a GitHub workflow checker would be called every 5 seconds (the crawl interval). The brief explicitly calls for per-type poll intervals. Storing lastCheckedAt on the block record in Stacks ensures the interval survives process restarts. The Spider compares Date.now() - lastCheckedAt against the block type's pollIntervalMs before calling the checker."

  - id: D14
    scope: [S3]
    category: api
    observable: true
    question: "What status does an unblocked engine transition to?"
    context: "When a checker returns true, the engine needs to become runnable again. The brief says the engine is 'restarted' — meaning it re-runs from scratch."
    options:
      a: "Transition to 'pending' — the normal runnable state. tryRun() picks it up on the next crawl."
      b: "Transition to a new 'unblocked' status that tryRun() also checks for"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "pending is the existing status that means 'ready to be evaluated for running.' Re-using it avoids adding yet another status. The engine re-enters the run queue naturally."

  # ── S4: Rig-level blocked status ───────────────────────────────────

  - id: D15
    scope: [S4]
    category: product
    observable: true
    question: "What is the exact condition for a rig to enter blocked status?"
    context: "The brief says 'when all forward progress in a rig is blocked.' This means no engines are running, no pending engines are runnable, but at least one engine is blocked."
    options:
      a: "No engine is 'running', no engine is 'pending' with all upstream completed, at least one engine is 'blocked'"
      b: "All non-completed engines are either 'blocked', 'cancelled', or 'pending' with at least one blocked ancestor — stricter definition"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "Option (a) is the natural formulation matching the brief. It's equivalent to: findRunnableEngine() returns null AND no engine has status 'running' AND at least one engine has status 'blocked'. This is the check already needed after an engine transitions to blocked."

  - id: D16
    scope: [S4]
    category: implementation
    observable: false
    question: "Where is the rig-blocked check performed?"
    context: "The rig can become blocked in two scenarios: (1) an engine returns blocked from tryRun(), (2) a running engine completes/collects and the only remaining non-completed engines are blocked + their dependents."
    options:
      a: "Inline check after every engine status change in tryRun() and tryCollect() — same places that check allCompleted"
      b: "Extract a helper isRigBlocked(rig) and call it from tryRun() and tryCollect()"
    selected: b
    analysis:
      recommendation: b
      confidence: high
      rationale: "The blocked check is needed in multiple code paths. Extracting it avoids duplication and makes the logic testable in isolation."

  - id: D17
    scope: [S4]
    category: implementation
    observable: false
    question: "When a blocked engine is unblocked (transitions to pending), does the rig automatically transition back to running?"
    context: "tryCheckBlocked() transitions the engine back to pending. The rig was blocked. Now it has a runnable engine."
    options:
      a: "Yes — tryCheckBlocked() patches both the engine status (pending) and rig status (running) in the same update"
      b: "No — the rig stays blocked until tryRun() picks up the now-pending engine"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "The rig must be 'running' for tryRun() to find it (tryRun queries status === 'running'). If the rig stays 'blocked', tryRun won't see it. So tryCheckBlocked must restore the rig to running when it unblocks an engine."

  # ── S5: CrawlResult observability ──────────────────────────────────

  - id: D18
    scope: [S5]
    category: api
    observable: true
    question: "What CrawlResult variants are added for block/unblock events?"
    context: "The brief asks for result variants at both engine and rig level. Current variants are engine-completed, engine-started, rig-spawned, rig-completed."
    options:
      a: "engine-blocked, engine-unblocked, rig-blocked — three new variants"
      b: "engine-blocked, engine-unblocked, rig-blocked, rig-unblocked — four new variants"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "rig-unblocked is redundant with engine-unblocked — when an engine is unblocked, the rig implicitly returns to running. The consumer can infer this. engine-blocked carries the block record. engine-unblocked signals a cleared condition. rig-blocked signals the rig has stalled. No need for a separate rig-unblocked variant."

  - id: D19
    scope: [S5]
    category: api
    observable: true
    question: "What payload do the new CrawlResult variants carry?"
    context: "Existing variants carry rigId plus engineId or writId. Block variants should carry enough for the caller to understand what happened."
    options:
      a: "engine-blocked: { rigId, engineId, blockType }; engine-unblocked: { rigId, engineId }; rig-blocked: { rigId, writId }"
      b: "engine-blocked: { rigId, engineId, blockType, condition }; engine-unblocked: { rigId, engineId }; rig-blocked: { rigId, writId }"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: medium
      rationale: "The condition payload may be large and opaque. blockType is sufficient for logging and observability. The full block record is accessible via rig-show if needed."

  # ── S6: Operator surface ───────────────────────────────────────────

  - id: D20
    scope: [S6]
    category: product
    observable: true
    question: "What is the resume API method signature?"
    context: "The brief says 'an operator can clear a block regardless of the checker, for any block type.' The tool is rig-resume."
    options:
      a: "resume(rigId: string, engineId: string): Promise<void> — requires both rig and engine"
      b: "resume(rigId: string, engineId?: string): Promise<void> — optional engineId resumes all blocked engines in the rig"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "Explicit is better than implicit. If a rig has multiple blocked engines, the operator should intentionally resume each one. Resuming all at once could have unintended consequences. They can call resume multiple times."

  - id: D21
    scope: [S6]
    category: product
    observable: true
    question: "What does the rig-resume tool do if the engine is not blocked?"
    context: "An operator might call resume on a non-blocked engine by mistake."
    options:
      a: "Throw an error — 'Engine X is not blocked'"
      b: "No-op — silently succeed"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "Fail-fast matches the codebase convention (show() throws if not found). Silent success hides mistakes."

  # ── S7: Prior block context ────────────────────────────────────────

  - id: D22
    scope: [S7]
    category: api
    observable: true
    question: "How is prior block context passed to the restarted engine?"
    context: "EngineRunContext currently has { engineId, upstream }. The engine needs to know about its prior block when restarted."
    options:
      a: "Add priorBlock?: BlockRecord to EngineRunContext — populated by Spider when engine restarts after unblock"
      b: "Add priorBlocks?: BlockRecord[] to EngineRunContext — array in case engine has been blocked multiple times"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "The engine only needs its most recent block to understand what just cleared. If it blocks again and gets restarted again, it gets that newer block as priorBlock. History is in the Stacks if anyone needs it. A single optional field is simpler."

  - id: D23
    scope: [S7]
    category: implementation
    observable: false
    question: "When is the block record cleared from the EngineInstance?"
    context: "When an engine is unblocked (transitions from blocked to pending), the Spider needs to preserve the block record for context but also clear it from the engine instance."
    options:
      a: "Clear the block field when transitioning to pending. Spider reads it before clearing and passes as priorBlock in the next run."
      b: "Keep the block field on the engine even after unblocking. Clear it when the engine starts running."
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "A pending engine should not have a block field — it's not blocked. The Spider reads the block, stores it in memory for the upcoming run() context, and clears it from the engine instance in the same patch. If the crawl process restarts between unblock and re-run, the priorBlock is lost — acceptable since it's advisory context, not critical state."

  # ── S8: Built-in block types ───────────────────────────────────────

  - id: D24
    scope: [S8]
    category: product
    observable: true
    question: "Which built-in block types ship, and where are they contributed?"
    context: "The brief lists: book updates, writ status, scheduled time, and at least one external-system example. They need to be registered via kit blockTypes contribution."
    options:
      a: "Ship in Spider's supportKit.blockTypes — parallel to how Spider contributes its engines"
      b: "Ship in a separate @shardworks/block-types package as a standalone kit"
      c: "Ship in a block-types/ directory within the Spider package, contributed via supportKit"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "The Spider already contributes engines via supportKit. Built-in block types are part of the same ecosystem. No need for a separate package at this scale. They live in the Spider's src/ alongside engines."

  - id: D25
    scope: [S8]
    category: product
    observable: true
    question: "Which specific built-in block types to implement?"
    context: "Brief says: book updates, writ status, scheduled time, and 'at least one external-system example.' The external example (GitHub workflow) requires API credentials which complicates a built-in."
    options:
      a: "writ-status, scheduled-time, book-updated — three internal types. Skip external example for now."
      b: "writ-status, scheduled-time, book-updated, http-poll — four types; http-poll is a generic external checker"
      c: "writ-status, scheduled-time, book-updated, github-workflow — four types as brief lists"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: medium
      rationale: "The three internal types cover the common cases and are self-contained (no API keys, no external dependencies). A generic http-poll or github-workflow type requires auth configuration that varies per guild. Better to ship a clean internal set and let guilds add external checkers via kits. The brief says 'at least one external-system example' but the mechanism for external block types is demonstrated by the framework itself — any kit can register one."

  # ── S9: Block condition schema validation ──────────────────────────

  - id: D26
    scope: [S9, S2]
    category: implementation
    observable: false
    question: "When does schema validation run, and what happens on failure?"
    context: "The brief says 'if validation fails, the engine is failed immediately (fail-fast).' The Spider validates when an engine returns blocked."
    options:
      a: "Spider calls blockType.validate(condition) after engine returns blocked. If it returns false, failEngine() with a clear error message."
      b: "Spider calls blockType.validate(condition) and if it throws, the throw message becomes the engine error."
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "Consistent with the brief's fail-fast language. validate() is a pure boolean check. The Spider provides the error message context: 'Engine returned blocked with type X but condition failed validation.' If validate is undefined on the block type, validation is skipped (per D5)."

  - id: D27
    scope: [S9, S1]
    category: implementation
    observable: false
    question: "What happens when an engine returns a blockType that isn't registered in the Fabricator?"
    context: "Brief says: 'catches typos, missing registrations, engines assuming apparatus that isn't installed.'"
    options:
      a: "Fail the engine immediately with error: 'Unknown block type: X'"
      b: "Warn and treat as unblockable — the engine stays blocked forever until manual resume"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "Brief explicitly says fail-fast on unregistered block types. A typo in a block type string should be caught immediately, not left as a silently stuck engine."

toolUseResult.content:
decisions:
  # ── S1: Engine blocked status ──────────────────────────────────────

  - id: D1
    scope: [S1]
    category: api
    observable: true
    question: "What shape does the blocked EngineRunResult variant take?"
    context: "EngineRunResult currently has two variants: { status: 'completed'; yields } and { status: 'launched'; sessionId }. The engine must declare a block type and condition payload."
    options:
      a: "{ status: 'blocked'; blockType: string; condition: unknown } — flat, parallel to existing variants"
      b: "{ status: 'blocked'; block: { type: string; condition: unknown } } — nested block record"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "Flat structure matches the existing variant style (status + payload fields). The Spider assembles the BlockRecord internally by adding blockedAt."

  - id: D2
    scope: [S1]
    category: api
    observable: true
    question: "What fields does the BlockRecord stored on EngineInstance contain?"
    context: "When an engine blocks, the Spider persists a block record on the EngineInstance. This record must contain enough for the checker to evaluate and for operator display."
    options:
      a: "{ type: string; condition: unknown; blockedAt: string } — minimal: block type, condition payload, timestamp"
      b: "{ type: string; condition: unknown; blockedAt: string; attempts: number } — adds a re-check counter"
      c: "{ type: string; condition: unknown; blockedAt: string; message?: string } — adds optional human-readable description from engine"
    selected: c
    analysis:
      recommendation: c
      confidence: medium
      rationale: "An optional message costs nothing but improves rig-show operator experience. The engine can explain what it's waiting for in human terms. Attempts tracking (b) can be added later if needed."

  - id: D3
    scope: [S1]
    category: implementation
    observable: false
    question: "When an engine returns blocked, does the Spider mark it running first then blocked, or directly pending→blocked?"
    context: "Currently tryRun() marks an engine 'running' before calling design.run(). If run() returns blocked, the engine was briefly 'running'. The Spider needs to then update to 'blocked'."
    options:
      a: "Keep existing pattern — mark running before run(), then update to blocked after. Two patches."
      b: "Keep existing pattern — mark running before run(), then update to blocked after. Single patch replaces 'running' with 'blocked'."
    selected: b
    analysis:
      recommendation: b
      confidence: high
      rationale: "The engine IS running while run() executes (it's synchronous from the Spider's perspective). When run() returns blocked, the Spider patches from running to blocked in one update. This is the same pattern as the launched/completed branches which also update after run()."

  - id: D4
    scope: [S1]
    category: implementation
    observable: false
    question: "Should failEngine() cancel blocked engines in addition to pending ones?"
    context: "failEngine() currently cancels all pending engines when one fails. Blocked engines are also non-terminal and can never complete if the rig fails."
    options:
      a: "Yes — cancel both pending and blocked engines on rig failure"
      b: "No — only cancel pending, leave blocked as-is"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "Blocked engines have no path to completion once the rig has failed. Leaving them blocked would be misleading. The failEngine() predicate should be e.status === 'pending' || e.status === 'blocked'."

  # ── S2: Block type registry ────────────────────────────────────────

  - id: D5
    scope: [S2]
    category: api
    observable: true
    question: "What is the BlockTypeDesign interface shape?"
    context: "The brief specifies a checker function, condition schema, and suggested poll interval. The checker signature is (condition) => Promise<boolean>. The schema needs to be machine-validatable."
    options:
      a: "{ id: string; check: (condition: unknown) => Promise<boolean>; conditionSchema: ZodSchema; pollIntervalMs?: number }"
      b: "{ id: string; check: (condition: unknown) => Promise<boolean>; conditionSchema?: ZodSchema; pollIntervalMs?: number } — schema optional"
      c: "{ id: string; check: (condition: unknown) => Promise<boolean>; validate?: (condition: unknown) => boolean; pollIntervalMs?: number } — custom validate function instead of Zod"
    selected: c
    analysis:
      recommendation: c
      confidence: high
      rationale: "The Fabricator doesn't depend on Zod today. Introducing a Zod dependency for condition schemas would break the clean dependency boundary. A validate function is more flexible — block type authors can use Zod internally if they want, or plain JS checks. The Spider calls validate(condition) at block time; if it returns false, the engine fails. If validate is not provided, any condition is accepted."

  - id: D6
    scope: [S2]
    category: api
    observable: true
    question: "Where does BlockTypeDesign live — Fabricator package or Spider package?"
    context: "EngineDesign lives in the Fabricator because the Fabricator is the capability catalog. Block types are also capabilities that kit authors register. But the Spider is the consumer. The Fabricator's role is to be the single registry for design-time artifacts."
    options:
      a: "Fabricator — BlockTypeDesign defined and registered in @shardworks/fabricator-apparatus, parallel to EngineDesign"
      b: "Spider — BlockTypeDesign defined and registered in @shardworks/spider-apparatus, since it's spider-specific"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "The Fabricator is the guild's capability catalog. Engine designs live there; block type designs are the same kind of thing — registered at startup, looked up at runtime. Kit authors import the type from the Fabricator. The Spider queries the Fabricator, same pattern as engine design lookup."

  - id: D7
    scope: [S2]
    category: implementation
    observable: false
    question: "How does the Fabricator scan blockTypes from kits?"
    context: "The Fabricator currently scans kit.engines using EngineRegistry.registerFromKit(). The same pattern works for blockTypes."
    options:
      a: "Add a parallel BlockTypeRegistry class with its own registerFromKit() scanning kit.blockTypes"
      b: "Extend EngineRegistry to handle both engines and blockTypes in a single class"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "Separate registries for separate concerns. The EngineRegistry and BlockTypeRegistry are small, parallel classes. Merging them would conflate two distinct type domains."

  - id: D8
    scope: [S2]
    category: api
    observable: true
    question: "What method does FabricatorApi expose for block type lookup?"
    context: "FabricatorApi currently has getEngineDesign(id). Need a parallel method for block types."
    options:
      a: "getBlockType(id: string): BlockTypeDesign | undefined"
      b: "getBlockTypeDesign(id: string): BlockTypeDesign | undefined — consistent naming with getEngineDesign"
    selected: b
    analysis:
      recommendation: b
      confidence: high
      rationale: "Consistent naming with getEngineDesign. Both return Design objects."

  - id: D9
    scope: [S2]
    category: api
    observable: true
    question: "What kit contribution field name is used for block type registrations?"
    context: "Engines use kit.engines. Tools use kit.tools. Need a field name for block type designs."
    options:
      a: "blockTypes — descriptive, clear"
      b: "blocks — shorter, but ambiguous (could mean blocked records)"
      c: "checkers — names the function, not the concept"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "blockTypes is unambiguous and parallel to the existing conventions. The Fabricator consumes: ['engines'] becomes consumes: ['engines', 'blockTypes']."

  # ── S3: Spider block-checking loop ─────────────────────────────────

  - id: D10
    scope: [S3]
    category: product
    observable: true
    question: "Where in the crawl priority order does tryCheckBlocked() run?"
    context: "Current order is collect > run > spawn. Each crawl() does one thing. The block check unblocks engines which then become runnable on a subsequent crawl."
    options:
      a: "collect > checkBlocked > run > spawn — block checks happen early, unblocked engines runnable next crawl"
      b: "collect > run > checkBlocked > spawn — block checks are lower priority than running new engines"
      c: "checkBlocked > collect > run > spawn — highest priority, unblocks as fast as possible"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "Collecting completed sessions should remain highest priority (it unblocks downstream engines). Block checking logically follows — it also unblocks engines. Running and spawning are lower priority. Option (a) gives unblocked engines a one-crawl delay before running, which is consistent with the one-action-per-crawl model."

  - id: D11
    scope: [S3]
    category: implementation
    observable: false
    question: "Does tryCheckBlocked() query running rigs, blocked rigs, or both?"
    context: "A rig with a blocked engine might still have status 'running' (other engines are still in progress). Or it might be 'blocked' (all progress halted). The checker needs to find blocked engines in both cases."
    options:
      a: "Query rigs with status IN ('running', 'blocked') and scan for blocked engines"
      b: "Query only blocked rigs — running rigs with blocked engines will eventually become blocked rigs when other engines complete"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "A running rig can have one blocked engine and one running engine. When the running engine completes, the blocked engine might be the only thing left. If we only check blocked rigs, the block check would be delayed until the rig transitions to blocked. Checking both ensures blocked engines are evaluated on every cycle regardless of rig status."

  - id: D12
    scope: [S3]
    category: implementation
    observable: false
    question: "When a checker throws an error, what happens to the blocked engine?"
    context: "Checkers are lightweight functions that may fail (network error, invalid condition, etc.). The brief doesn't specify error handling."
    options:
      a: "Log warning, skip — engine stays blocked. Retry next cycle."
      b: "Fail the engine — checker error is an unrecoverable configuration problem"
      c: "Log warning, skip, increment a retry counter — fail after N consecutive errors"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: medium
      rationale: "Checkers are external-facing (HTTP calls, file system checks). Transient failures are expected. Failing the engine on a network blip would be too aggressive. Logging and retrying next cycle is resilient. The operator can use rig-resume if the checker is permanently broken."

  - id: D13
    scope: [S3]
    category: implementation
    observable: false
    question: "Does tryCheckBlocked() respect the block type's suggested pollIntervalMs?"
    context: "The brief mentions a suggested poll interval per block type to avoid hammering external APIs. But the crawl loop already has its own pollIntervalMs (default 5000ms)."
    options:
      a: "Yes — track lastCheckedAt per blocked engine, skip if less than pollIntervalMs since last check"
      b: "No — check every cycle. The crawl loop's own interval is the throttle."
      c: "Yes — but store lastCheckedAt on the block record in the EngineInstance, persisted in Stacks"
    selected: c
    analysis:
      recommendation: c
      confidence: high
      rationale: "Without per-block-type throttling, a GitHub workflow checker would be called every 5 seconds (the crawl interval). The brief explicitly calls for per-type poll intervals. Storing lastCheckedAt on the block record in Stacks ensures the interval survives process restarts. The Spider compares Date.now() - lastCheckedAt against the block type's pollIntervalMs before calling the checker."

  - id: D14
    scope: [S3]
    category: api
    observable: true
    question: "What status does an unblocked engine transition to?"
    context: "When a checker returns true, the engine needs to become runnable again. The brief says the engine is 'restarted' — meaning it re-runs from scratch."
    options:
      a: "Transition to 'pending' — the normal runnable state. tryRun() picks it up on the next crawl."
      b: "Transition to a new 'unblocked' status that tryRun() also checks for"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "pending is the existing status that means 'ready to be evaluated for running.' Re-using it avoids adding yet another status. The engine re-enters the run queue naturally."

  # ── S4: Rig-level blocked status ───────────────────────────────────

  - id: D15
    scope: [S4]
    category: product
    observable: true
    question: "What is the exact condition for a rig to enter blocked status?"
    context: "The brief says 'when all forward progress in a rig is blocked.' This means no engines are running, no pending engines are runnable, but at least one engine is blocked."
    options:
      a: "No engine is 'running', no engine is 'pending' with all upstream completed, at least one engine is 'blocked'"
      b: "All non-completed engines are either 'blocked', 'cancelled', or 'pending' with at least one blocked ancestor — stricter definition"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "Option (a) is the natural formulation matching the brief. It's equivalent to: findRunnableEngine() returns null AND no engine has status 'running' AND at least one engine has status 'blocked'. This is the check already needed after an engine transitions to blocked."

  - id: D16
    scope: [S4]
    category: implementation
    observable: false
    question: "Where is the rig-blocked check performed?"
    context: "The rig can become blocked in two scenarios: (1) an engine returns blocked from tryRun(), (2) a running engine completes/collects and the only remaining non-completed engines are blocked + their dependents."
    options:
      a: "Inline check after every engine status change in tryRun() and tryCollect() — same places that check allCompleted"
      b: "Extract a helper isRigBlocked(rig) and call it from tryRun() and tryCollect()"
    selected: b
    analysis:
      recommendation: b
      confidence: high
      rationale: "The blocked check is needed in multiple code paths. Extracting it avoids duplication and makes the logic testable in isolation."

  - id: D17
    scope: [S4]
    category: implementation
    observable: false
    question: "When a blocked engine is unblocked (transitions to pending), does the rig automatically transition back to running?"
    context: "tryCheckBlocked() transitions the engine back to pending. The rig was blocked. Now it has a runnable engine."
    options:
      a: "Yes — tryCheckBlocked() patches both the engine status (pending) and rig status (running) in the same update"
      b: "No — the rig stays blocked until tryRun() picks up the now-pending engine"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "The rig must be 'running' for tryRun() to find it (tryRun queries status === 'running'). If the rig stays 'blocked', tryRun won't see it. So tryCheckBlocked must restore the rig to running when it unblocks an engine."

  # ── S5: CrawlResult observability ──────────────────────────────────

  - id: D18
    scope: [S5]
    category: api
    observable: true
    question: "What CrawlResult variants are added for block/unblock events?"
    context: "The brief asks for result variants at both engine and rig level. Current variants are engine-completed, engine-started, rig-spawned, rig-completed."
    options:
      a: "engine-blocked, engine-unblocked, rig-blocked — three new variants"
      b: "engine-blocked, engine-unblocked, rig-blocked, rig-unblocked — four new variants"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "rig-unblocked is redundant with engine-unblocked — when an engine is unblocked, the rig implicitly returns to running. The consumer can infer this. engine-blocked carries the block record. engine-unblocked signals a cleared condition. rig-blocked signals the rig has stalled. No need for a separate rig-unblocked variant."

  - id: D19
    scope: [S5]
    category: api
    observable: true
    question: "What payload do the new CrawlResult variants carry?"
    context: "Existing variants carry rigId plus engineId or writId. Block variants should carry enough for the caller to understand what happened."
    options:
      a: "engine-blocked: { rigId, engineId, blockType }; engine-unblocked: { rigId, engineId }; rig-blocked: { rigId, writId }"
      b: "engine-blocked: { rigId, engineId, blockType, condition }; engine-unblocked: { rigId, engineId }; rig-blocked: { rigId, writId }"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: medium
      rationale: "The condition payload may be large and opaque. blockType is sufficient for logging and observability. The full block record is accessible via rig-show if needed."

  # ── S6: Operator surface ───────────────────────────────────────────

  - id: D20
    scope: [S6]
    category: product
    observable: true
    question: "What is the resume API method signature?"
    context: "The brief says 'an operator can clear a block regardless of the checker, for any block type.' The tool is rig-resume."
    options:
      a: "resume(rigId: string, engineId: string): Promise<void> — requires both rig and engine"
      b: "resume(rigId: string, engineId?: string): Promise<void> — optional engineId resumes all blocked engines in the rig"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "Explicit is better than implicit. If a rig has multiple blocked engines, the operator should intentionally resume each one. Resuming all at once could have unintended consequences. They can call resume multiple times."

      b: "No-op — silently succeed"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "Fail-fast matches the codebase convention (show() throws if not found). Silent success hides mistakes."

    options:
      a: "Add priorBlock?: BlockRecord to EngineRunContext — populated by Spider when engine restarts after unblock"

  - id: D24

    category: product

      b: "writ-status, scheduled-time, book-updated, http-poll — four types; http-poll is a generic external checker"
      c: "writ-status, scheduled-time, book-updated, github-workflow — four types as brief lists"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: medium
      rationale: "The three internal types cover the common cases and are self-contained (no API keys, no external dependencies). A generic http-poll or github-workflow type requires auth configuration that varies per guild. Better to ship a clean internal set and let guilds add external checkers via kits. The brief says 'at least one external-system example' but the mechanism for external block types is demonstrated by the framework itself — any kit can register one."

  # ── S9: Block condition schema validation ──────────────────────────

  - id: D26
    scope: [S9, S2]
    category: implementation
    observable: false
    question: "When does schema validation run, and what happens on failure?"
    context: "The brief says 'if validation fails, the engine is failed immediately (fail-fast).' The Spider validates when an engine returns blocked."
    options:
      a: "Spider calls blockType.validate(condition) after engine returns blocked. If it returns false, failEngine() with a clear error message."
      b: "Spider calls blockType.validate(condition) and if it throws, the throw message becomes the engine error."
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "Consistent with the brief's fail-fast language. validate() is a pure boolean check. The Spider provides the error message context: 'Engine returned blocked with type X but condition failed validation.' If validate is undefined on the block type, validation is skipped (per D5)."

  - id: D27
    scope: [S9, S1]
    category: implementation
    observable: false
    question: "What happens when an engine returns a blockType that isn't registered in the Fabricator?"
    context: "Brief says: 'catches typos, missing registrations, engines assuming apparatus that isn't installed.'"
    options:
      a: "Fail the engine immediately with error: 'Unknown block type: X'"
      b: "Warn and treat as unblockable — the engine stays blocked forever until manual resume"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "Brief explicitly says fail-fast on unregistered block types. A typo in a block type string should be caught immediately, not left as a silently stuck engine."

input.content:
# Observations

## Refactoring opportunities skipped for scope

1. **`tryRun()` and `tryCollect()` share rig-status-check logic.** Both functions have inline `allCompleted` checks that determine if a rig should transition to `completed`. With `blocked` added, both also need `isRigBlocked()` checks. This is an opportunity to extract a `resolveRigStatus(engines) → RigStatus` helper, but doing so as a refactor risks destabilizing existing tests. Decision D16 addresses the `isRigBlocked` extraction; the broader `resolveRigStatus` refactor is left for later.

2. **`failEngine()` hardcodes the cancellation predicate.** The function has `if (e.status === 'pending')` as its cancel condition. Adding `|| e.status === 'blocked'` works but the function is accumulating special cases. A future cleanup could use a set of "non-terminal, non-running" statuses.

3. **`tryRun()` queries ALL running rigs every cycle.** `tryCheckBlocked()` will query running + blocked rigs. As rig counts grow, these linear scans may benefit from pagination or a priority queue. Not a problem at current scale but worth noting for future optimization.

## Suboptimal conventions followed for consistency

1. **Condition validation uses a function instead of Zod.** The Fabricator has no Zod dependency. Introducing one would break the clean dependency boundary. The `validate?: (condition: unknown) => boolean` pattern is less ergonomic than Zod schemas but maintains the Fabricator's zero-dependency stance. Block type authors can use Zod internally and wrap it in validate(). (Decision D5.)

2. **`lastCheckedAt` stored on the block record in Stacks.** This means every checker evaluation that returns false triggers a Stacks write (to update lastCheckedAt). An in-memory cache would be cheaper but wouldn't survive process restarts. The Stacks write is a patch on an existing document (the rig's engines array), so it piggybacks existing I/O. Acceptable overhead for correctness. (Decision D13.)

## Doc/code discrepancies found during inventory

1. **`docs/architecture/apparatus/spider.md` tool list** says `supportKit.tools: [crawlOneTool, crawlContinualTool]` but actual code has five tools including rigShowTool, rigListTool, rigForWritTool. The doc is stale.

2. **`docs/guides/building-engines.md`** describes a completely different engine concept (Clockworks handlers with standing orders and `nexus-engine.json`). This is NOT the same system as `EngineDesign` objects registered with the Fabricator. No Clockworks apparatus exists in the current codebase. The guide either describes a pre-apparatus architecture or an unbuilt system.

3. **`docs/architecture/apparatus/spider.md` static graph** describes `yields: null` as initial value on EngineInstances, but actual code uses `yields?: unknown` (optional, not set at spawn time).

4. **`docs/architecture/apparatus/spider.md` CDC handler** shows structured resolution string using `sealYields.sealedCommit`, but code uses `JSON.stringify(sealEngine.yields)`.

## Potential risks in adjacent code

1. **Process restart between unblock and re-run.** If the Spider process restarts after `tryCheckBlocked()` unblocks an engine (sets it to `pending`) but before `tryRun()` re-runs it, the `priorBlock` context is lost (it's held in memory per D23). The engine still runs correctly — it just doesn't know it was previously blocked. This is acceptable for advisory context but worth documenting for engine authors.

2. **Checker functions running within the crawl loop.** Checkers are expected to be lightweight (HTTP calls, Stacks reads). A slow or hanging checker would block the entire crawl cycle. The brief acknowledges this is the baseline; the crawl loop is already synchronous (one action per call). A future improvement could add per-checker timeouts.

3. **`crawl-continual` observability for blocked rigs.** When all rigs are blocked and no writs are ready, `crawl()` returns null (idle). The `crawl-continual` loop increments idleCount. If `maxIdleCycles` is set, the loop may stop even though there are blocked rigs that could unblock. This is the current idle detection behavior; blocked rigs should probably not count as "idle" since work is pending. However, addressing this would change `crawl-continual` semantics — noted for future consideration.

content:
-
MODE: ANALYST

Here is the brief:

# Implement ability to block engines on external conditions

## Problem

Currently, once a rig is created the Spider starts each engine as it becomes ready, and engines can only "succeed" (with yields) or "fail" (causing the whole rig to fail). There is no way for an engine to signal that it has encountered an external condition it cannot resolve itself and must wait for.

## Desired behavior

Engines should be able to enter a **blocked** state — signaling to the Spider that the engine cannot proceed, but has not failed. The engine terminates cleanly and declares *what* it is waiting for using a structured block record. When the blocking condition clears, the Spider detects the change and restarts the engine, which may then run to completion, fail, or block again.

"Blocked" is the correct term: the engine wants to proceed but can't until a condition is met..

## Example blocking conditions (not exhaustive)

- Feedback needed from a human operator (e.g., a Stacks Book is written to)
- Waiting for an external system (e.g., a GitHub Actions workflow completes, a Vercel deployment finishes)
- Waiting for a time-based condition (e.g., entering a deployment window)
- Waiting for an internal dependency (e.g., another writ reaches a target status)

## Architecture: Block types and condition checkers

The key architectural decision: **engines declare what they're waiting for, but do not implement the checking logic.** Checking whether a block condition has cleared is handled by lightweight, non-LLM condition checkers registered as guild apparatus.

### Why this approach

The naive alternative — Spider polls blocked engines by restarting them, and engines self-check their conditions — is expensive. Most block checks are trivially answerable ("has this book been updated?" "is this workflow done?" "is it after 2pm?") but if the engine protocol requires spinning up a full agent session to check, you burn LLM tokens on what should be a `curl` call. Separating the checking concern from the engine also means common block types (GitHub workflow, scheduled time, book update) are write-once/use-everywhere rather than reimplemented in every engine.

### Block types as registered components

Block types are registered with the guild by apparatus, similar to engines and tools. A block type registration includes:

- A **checker** — a lightweight function/script that evaluates whether the condition has cleared. Signature: `(condition) => boolean`. No LLM involved.
- A **condition schema** — describes the shape of the condition payload the engine must provide. Enables validation at block time.
- A suggested **poll interval** — hint to the Spider for how frequently to check this type (don't hammer GitHub every 2 seconds; check timestamps every 30 seconds).

### Block records

When an engine exits blocked, it provides a block type and a structured condition payload. The Spider validates the block type exists in the guild and the condition matches the type's schema. If validation fails, the engine is failed immediately (fail-fast — catches typos, missing registrations, engines assuming apparatus that isn't installed).

Valid block records are persisted and the engine enters the `blocked` status.

### Condition checking: Spider-owned

The Spider owns the block-checking loop as a natural extension of its rig-tending responsibilities. On each crawl cycle, for each blocked engine, the Spider runs the registered checker for that block's type. If the checker returns true, the block is cleared and the engine is transitioned back to a runnable state.

For internal conditions (book updates, writ status changes), the system can use CDC/events to clear blocks immediately rather than waiting for the next poll tick. But polling is the baseline mechanism — events are an optimization layered on top.

### Engine author experience

An engine author's responsibility is minimal:

1. **Detect** the blocking condition during engine execution
2. **Declare** what they're waiting for: a block type string and a condition payload matching that type's schema
3. That's it. No check logic. No re-check on startup.

When the engine is restarted after unblocking, it receives context about the prior block (so it can pick up where it left off if needed).

### Checker author experience

A block type author writes:

1. A checker function: `(condition: T) => Promise<boolean>` — returns true if the condition has cleared
2. A condition schema for validation
3. Registers it as guild apparatus

Built-in block types should ship with the framework for common cases: book updates, writ status, scheduled time, and at least one external-system example.

## Rig-level blocked status

When all forward progress in a rig is blocked (the only non-completed engines are blocked, plus their pending dependents), the rig itself transitions to `blocked` status. This gives operators clear queryability ("show me all blocked rigs") and prevents the crawl loop from wasting cycles on rigs that can't make progress. The rig transitions back to `running` when any engine is unblocked.

## Operator surface

- `rig-list` should support filtering by `blocked` status
- `rig-show` should surface block metadata (what the engine is waiting for, how long it's been blocked)
- A `rig-resume` tool provides manual override — an operator can clear a block regardless of the checker, for any block type

## CrawlResult observability

The crawl loop should emit result variants for block and unblock events at both engine and rig level, so callers of `crawl()` (including `crawl-continual`) can observe these state transitions.
`s`

---
